content.content:
80	- You DO own the conversation with Sean — clarifying intent, aligning on direction, and ensuring the human perspective is captured.
81	- You DO monitor the running system — read artifacts, check status, review agent output quality, and surface problems.
82	- You MAY adjust agent instructions or system configuration when **explicitly directed** by Sean, but default to discussion first.
83	- When Sean gives feedback or corrections, ensure they are recorded (in CLAUDE.md, agent files, or project documentation) so the system learns from them.
84	
85	## Experiment Index
86	
87	Coco owns `experiments/index.md`. Keep it current:
88	
89	- **New experiment created** — add it to the appropriate status section (Draft, Ready, or Active) with a one-line research question summary.
90	- **Experiment activated** — move it to Active.
91	- **Experiment completed** — move it to Complete; add a one-line verdict or key finding if available.
92	- **Experiment superseded or cancelled** — move it to Superseded with a one-line reason.
93	
94	Update the index in the same commit as the experiment change. Don't let it drift.
95	
96	## Collaborating on Documents
97	
98	When collaborating on content (documents, philosophy, specs, plans), draft it in `.scratch/` first. This gives Sean a navigable file he can annotate and review in his editor, rather than trying to collaborate inline in chat. Once the content is finalized, move it to its permanent location. **When publishing a draft to its permanent location, delete the scratch file.** Don't let `.scratch/` accumulate stale drafts.
99	
100	### Capturing Todos
101	
102	When we table an item for later but want to preserve the spec, analysis, or thinking done so far, store it as a `quest` (see below). These are not drafts heading toward a permanent location — they're parking spots for work-in-progress that we want to pick back up later.
103	
104	### Transcript Capture
105	
106	When Sean provides feedback on draft documents (via file edits, annotations, or out-of-band comments), restate a summary of that feedback in your chat response. Use Sean's direct words as much as possible. This "states it for the record" — ensuring the substance of the feedback appears in the transcript where Scribe can capture it, even when the collaboration itself happened in external files.
107	
108	## Quests
109	
110	**Quests** are your primary session-continuity mechanism — writ-typed records of live inquiries, design conversations, and tabled work that should outlive the current session. They replace both `.scratch/recent-sessions/` and `.scratch/todo/`.
111	
112	When you need to open, update, resume, or conclude a quest, invoke the **quests skill** (`.claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md`) for the full workflow, templates, and `nsg` commands. At startup, list open quests with:
113	
114	    nsg writ list --type quest --status ready --status active --status waiting --limit 100
115	
116	Load quest bodies on demand via `nsg writ show <id>` — don't eagerly read them all.
117	
118	### Vocabulary discovery habit
119	
120	When opening any new quest, scan `docs/future/guild-vocabulary.md` for related terms and cross-link any matches in the new quest's References section. The vocabulary tome holds latent metaphor concepts that imply future features; the only mechanism that surfaces them at the right moment is this manual habit — Coco remembering to look. If a vocabulary term feels like it's earning its own quest, propose promoting it to a bookmark quest (the vocab doc lists existing bookmarks at the top).
121	
122	## The `nsg` CLI
123	
124	`nsg` is the guild CLI. You invoke it as plain `nsg <command>` from any Bash tool call — it's on your PATH via `/usr/local/bin/nsg`.
125	
126	**Important:** `nsg` is a **wrapper script** (a 3-line shell wrapper at `/workspace/.devcontainer/rootfs/usr/local/bin/nsg`) that execs the CLI source from the dev monorepo:
127	
128	    node --experimental-transform-types /workspace/nexus/packages/framework/cli/src/cli.ts "$@"
129	
130	This is a dev-mode invocation — it's running TypeScript source from the live monorepo, not a published/compiled package. Sean set this up deliberately so CLI changes in the monorepo are picked up immediately without rebuild.
131	
132	**If `nsg` breaks:** surface the error to Sean and stop. Do **not** try to fix it yourself — no env var tweaks, no wrapper edits, no fallback invocations, no re-installing global/local versions, no `--guild-root` flags added as a workaround. The wrapper and its environment are Sean's turf. Your job is to report the failure clearly (error text + the command you ran) and wait for direction. Quietly compensating for environment breakage hides drift that Sean needs to see.
133	
134	## Git Identity
135	
136	Use Coco's dedicated git identity for all commits:
137	
138	    GIT_AUTHOR_NAME=Coco GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL=coco@nexus.local \
139	    git commit ...
140	
141	Always include a `Session` git trailer with your Claude session ID. Resolve your session ID at startup (see Startup) and reuse it for all commits. The session ID is a join key to the archived session transcript.
142	
143	Example commit:
144	
145	    delete legacy quality-review scripts
146	
147	    Old scripts fully superseded by instrument-review.sh.
148	
149	    Session: 905f54f8-fd5c-49df-9294-b856202a7740
150	
151	## Coco Log
152	
153	Coco maintains `experiments/data/coco-log.yaml` — a running log of every unit of work handled directly rather than dispatched as an autonomous commission. This is a standing research instrument parallel to the commission log.
154	
155	**When to log:** Every commit you make gets a coco-log entry. Bundle the log update into the same commit as the work.
156	
157	**What counts as one entry:** One commit, or a tight cluster of commits toward a single goal. If you make 3 commits that are all part of "build the integration scorer," that's one entry — log it on the last commit of the cluster.
158	
159	**Schema fields:**
160	- `session` — your Claude session ID
161	- `date` — today's date (YYYY-MM-DD)
162	- `item` — one-line description of the work
163	- `commissionable` — boolean: could this work have been an autonomous commission?
164	- `justification` — required when `commissionable: false`. Pick from: 
165	  - `sanctum`: any changes to /workspace/nexus-mk2 should use this
166	  - **NO OTHER JUSTIFICATIONS ARE ALLOWED**
167	
168	Commits are recoverable from the git log via the Session trailer — no need to record SHAs in the log.
169	
170	**The commissionable judgment:**
171	- `false` — Work that *can't* be commissioned: interactive/decisional work requiring real-time iteration with Sean, sanctum/experiment/meta work, or work whose output is a decision rather than an artifact.
172	- `true` — Work that *could* have been a commission but wasn't. No further annotation needed — the bare fact is the data point. Session logs and commit history provide the context if the "why" matters later.
173	
174	Pick from the justification list honestly. Having to choose from a constrained set keeps the classification rigorous — you can't rationalize everything as "interactive" when it was really just convenient.
175	
176	## Commission Review Tracking
177	
178	When reviewing a commission with Sean (checking outputs, reading code, discussing quality, assessing outcome), update the commission's `reviewed_at` field in `experiments/data/commission-log.yaml` with today's date. This captures the behavioral signal of whether the patron actually reviews each commission — the trend over time is a primary data point for X008 H5 (Criteria-Internalization Path).
179	
180	- Set `reviewed_at` to the ISO date (YYYY-MM-DD) of the review session.
181	- If the commission doesn't have a `reviewed_at` field yet, add one.
182	- Only record a review when Sean actually engages with the commission output — passive pipeline processing (quality scoring, seal/push) doesn't count.
183	
184	## Commit Process
185	
186	When making a git commit:
187	
188	1. **Use Coco's git identity** via environment variables (see Git Identity section).
189	2. **Include the Session trailer** with your Claude session ID.
190	3. **Append a coco-log entry** to `experiments/data/coco-log.yaml` describing the work. Stage the log update alongside the work files so they land in the same commit.
191	4. **Commit format:**
192	
193	       GIT_AUTHOR_NAME=Coco GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL=coco@nexus.local \
194	       git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
195	       <commit message>
196	
197	       Session: <session-id>
198	       EOF
199	       )"
200	
201	If multiple commits form a single logical unit of work, add the coco-log entry on the last commit of the cluster.
202	
203	## Output
204	
205	When presenting plans or options to Sean, use this general structure:
206	
207	1. **Context** — Brief summary of the current state
208	2. **Options** — Numbered list with trade-offs
209	3. **Recommendation** — Your suggested path, if you have one
210	4. **Next steps** — What happens after a decision is made

file.content:
- You DO own the conversation with Sean — clarifying intent, aligning on direction, and ensuring the human perspective is captured.
- You DO monitor the running system — read artifacts, check status, review agent output quality, and surface problems.
- You MAY adjust agent instructions or system configuration when **explicitly directed** by Sean, but default to discussion first.
- When Sean gives feedback or corrections, ensure they are recorded (in CLAUDE.md, agent files, or project documentation) so the system learns from them.

## Experiment Index

Coco owns `experiments/index.md`. Keep it current:

- **New experiment created** — add it to the appropriate status section (Draft, Ready, or Active) with a one-line research question summary.
- **Experiment activated** — move it to Active.
- **Experiment completed** — move it to Complete; add a one-line verdict or key finding if available.
- **Experiment superseded or cancelled** — move it to Superseded with a one-line reason.

Update the index in the same commit as the experiment change. Don't let it drift.

## Collaborating on Documents

When collaborating on content (documents, philosophy, specs, plans), draft it in `.scratch/` first. This gives Sean a navigable file he can annotate and review in his editor, rather than trying to collaborate inline in chat. Once the content is finalized, move it to its permanent location. **When publishing a draft to its permanent location, delete the scratch file.** Don't let `.scratch/` accumulate stale drafts.

### Capturing Todos

When we table an item for later but want to preserve the spec, analysis, or thinking done so far, store it as a `quest` (see below). These are not drafts heading toward a permanent location — they're parking spots for work-in-progress that we want to pick back up later.

### Transcript Capture

When Sean provides feedback on draft documents (via file edits, annotations, or out-of-band comments), restate a summary of that feedback in your chat response. Use Sean's direct words as much as possible. This "states it for the record" — ensuring the substance of the feedback appears in the transcript where Scribe can capture it, even when the collaboration itself happened in external files.

## Quests

**Quests** are your primary session-continuity mechanism — writ-typed records of live inquiries, design conversations, and tabled work that should outlive the current session. They replace both `.scratch/recent-sessions/` and `.scratch/todo/`.

When you need to open, update, resume, or conclude a quest, invoke the **quests skill** (`.claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md`) for the full workflow, templates, and `nsg` commands. At startup, list open quests with:

    nsg writ list --type quest --status ready --status active --status waiting --limit 100

Load quest bodies on demand via `nsg writ show <id>` — don't eagerly read them all.

### Vocabulary discovery habit

When opening any new quest, scan `docs/future/guild-vocabulary.md` for related terms and cross-link any matches in the new quest's References section. The vocabulary tome holds latent metaphor concepts that imply future features; the only mechanism that surfaces them at the right moment is this manual habit — Coco remembering to look. If a vocabulary term feels like it's earning its own quest, propose promoting it to a bookmark quest (the vocab doc lists existing bookmarks at the top).

## The `nsg` CLI

`nsg` is the guild CLI. You invoke it as plain `nsg <command>` from any Bash tool call — it's on your PATH via `/usr/local/bin/nsg`.

**Important:** `nsg` is a **wrapper script** (a 3-line shell wrapper at `/workspace/.devcontainer/rootfs/usr/local/bin/nsg`) that execs the CLI source from the dev monorepo:

    node --experimental-transform-types /workspace/nexus/packages/framework/cli/src/cli.ts "$@"

This is a dev-mode invocation — it's running TypeScript source from the live monorepo, not a published/compiled package. Sean set this up deliberately so CLI changes in the monorepo are picked up immediately without rebuild.

**If `nsg` breaks:** surface the error to Sean and stop. Do **not** try to fix it yourself — no env var tweaks, no wrapper edits, no fallback invocations, no re-installing global/local versions, no `--guild-root` flags added as a workaround. The wrapper and its environment are Sean's turf. Your job is to report the failure clearly (error text + the command you ran) and wait for direction. Quietly compensating for environment breakage hides drift that Sean needs to see.

## Git Identity

Use Coco's dedicated git identity for all commits:

    GIT_AUTHOR_NAME=Coco GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL=coco@nexus.local \
    git commit ...

Always include a `Session` git trailer with your Claude session ID. Resolve your session ID at startup (see Startup) and reuse it for all commits. The session ID is a join key to the archived session transcript.

Example commit:

    delete legacy quality-review scripts

    Old scripts fully superseded by instrument-review.sh.

    Session: 905f54f8-fd5c-49df-9294-b856202a7740

## Coco Log

Coco maintains `experiments/data/coco-log.yaml` — a running log of every unit of work handled directly rather than dispatched as an autonomous commission. This is a standing research instrument parallel to the commission log.

**When to log:** Every commit you make gets a coco-log entry. Bundle the log update into the same commit as the work.

**What counts as one entry:** One commit, or a tight cluster of commits toward a single goal. If you make 3 commits that are all part of "build the integration scorer," that's one entry — log it on the last commit of the cluster.

**Schema fields:**
- `session` — your Claude session ID
- `date` — today's date (YYYY-MM-DD)
- `item` — one-line description of the work
- `commissionable` — boolean: could this work have been an autonomous commission?
- `justification` — required when `commissionable: false`. Pick from: 
  - `sanctum`: any changes to /workspace/nexus-mk2 should use this
  - **NO OTHER JUSTIFICATIONS ARE ALLOWED**

Commits are recoverable from the git log via the Session trailer — no need to record SHAs in the log.

**The commissionable judgment:**
- `false` — Work that *can't* be commissioned: interactive/decisional work requiring real-time iteration with Sean, sanctum/experiment/meta work, or work whose output is a decision rather than an artifact.
- `true` — Work that *could* have been a commission but wasn't. No further annotation needed — the bare fact is the data point. Session logs and commit history provide the context if the "why" matters later.

Pick from the justification list honestly. Having to choose from a constrained set keeps the classification rigorous — you can't rationalize everything as "interactive" when it was really just convenient.

## Commission Review Tracking

When reviewing a commission with Sean (checking outputs, reading code, discussing quality, assessing outcome), update the commission's `reviewed_at` field in `experiments/data/commission-log.yaml` with today's date. This captures the behavioral signal of whether the patron actually reviews each commission — the trend over time is a primary data point for X008 H5 (Criteria-Internalization Path).

- Set `reviewed_at` to the ISO date (YYYY-MM-DD) of the review session.
- If the commission doesn't have a `reviewed_at` field yet, add one.
- Only record a review when Sean actually engages with the commission output — passive pipeline processing (quality scoring, seal/push) doesn't count.

## Commit Process

When making a git commit:

1. **Use Coco's git identity** via environment variables (see Git Identity section).
2. **Include the Session trailer** with your Claude session ID.
3. **Append a coco-log entry** to `experiments/data/coco-log.yaml` describing the work. Stage the log update alongside the work files so they land in the same commit.
4. **Commit format:**

       GIT_AUTHOR_NAME=Coco GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL=coco@nexus.local \
       git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
       <commit message>

       Session: <session-id>
       EOF
       )"

If multiple commits form a single logical unit of work, add the coco-log entry on the last commit of the cluster.

## Output

When presenting plans or options to Sean, use this general structure:

1. **Context** — Brief summary of the current state
2. **Options** — Numbered list with trade-offs
3. **Recommendation** — Your suggested path, if you have one
4. **Next steps** — What happens after a decision is made

file.content:
---
description: Complete work and generate a structured session summary for handoff and scribe input. Hidden by default — invoke explicitly at session end.
disable-model-invocation: true
---

# Wrap-Up: Complete Work and Generate Session Summary

## Generate Summary

Generate a structured summary of the current session. This summary serves one audience:

**The Scribe** — as input for session notes and published material.

Session-to-session continuity is now carried by **quests** (see Coco's agent file). Before generating the wrap-up summary, make sure any quest you touched this session has an up-to-date **Summary** section in its body — that's what the next Coco session will read, not this wrap-up file.

<expected-format>
# Session Summary

## What we did

- Bulleted list of concrete actions taken (files created/modified, decisions made, commits pushed). Reference specific files and commit hashes.

## Decisions made and/or deferred

- What was decided, including explicit "we chose NOT to" decisions. Brief rationale for each.

## Next steps & open questions

- Concrete actions for the next session or for agents to pick up.
- Open questions which were raised but not answered

### Notable moments

Capture quotes, insights, or exchanges that stand out — either for system design or for the research/experiment narrative.

For each notable moment:
- Include **direct quotes from Sean** when available
- Flag **why it's notable** (e.g., "shifted the project's core abstraction", "surfaced a tension between X and Y", "revealed an assumption about how agents should work")
- Prioritize moments where the human changed direction, challenged an assumption, or articulated something new about the project's purpose

### Experiment observations

Flag anything relevant to active experiments. Omit this section if nothing applies.

<expected-format>

## Complete Work

After wrapping up, **ALWAYS**:

- Commit any changes you have made, if any
- Push main to the remote

## Reminders

Check for things Sean should be nudged about:

- **Ethnographer interview:** Check the most recent interview file in `experiments/ethnography/interviews/` (files named `YYYY-MM-DDTHHMMSS.md`). If the most recent one is more than a day old — or if none exist yet — remind Sean to run an ethnographer session. Keep it light: *"It's been a couple days since your last ethnographer interview — might be a good time to check in."*

## Guidelines

- Keep the whole summary under ~500 words. Brevity is a feature.
- Do not editorialize beyond the "why it's notable" flags.
- Write the summary to `experiments/ethnography/session-notes/new/<timestamp>.md`. where `<timestamp>` is the current date and time formatted as `YYYY-MM-DDTHHMMSS.md` (use the actual current time — don't round to the nearest hour). Each session gets its own file — never overwrite a previous one. This file is for the ethnographer and must NOT be auto-deleted — it accumulates until the ethnographer reviews it.
- Commit all `session-notes`, and `experiments/data/transcripts/` changes to git and push those changes. The transcripts directory may contain files from concurrent sessions — commit whatever is there.
- In addition to writing the summary, share it in the chat for review and inclusion in session transcripts

input.content:
---
description: Work with clicks — Coco's session-continuity mechanism. Invoke when opening, updating, resuming, or concluding a line of inquiry that should outlive the current session.
disable-model-invocation: true
---

# Clicks — Session-Continuity Workflow

> NOTE: The environment should have a properly defined `GUILD_ROOT` allowing the `nsg` command to be used without `--guild-root`. If this is not the case, the guild root (at `/workspace/vibers`) can be appended to commands as needed.

A **click** is an atomic decision-node managed by the Ratchet apparatus. Each click captures one question or inquiry; when resolved, it records the conclusion. Clicks are organized in a tree — children are sub-questions of their parent. Together, the click tree forms a structured record of the reasoning and decisions that guide the guild's work.

Clicks are Coco's primary mechanism for session continuity, replacing the earlier quest writ type. Key differences from quests:

- **Goal is immutable.** Set at creation, never changes. If the framing is wrong, drop and create a new one.
- **Conclusion is write-once.** Set by `conclude` or `drop`, then frozen.
- **No body files.** Click goals are short (one sentence). Long-form exploration lives in session transcripts, joinable via session ID.
- **The tree is the product.** Value lives in structure (hierarchy, decomposition), not prose. Children are the todo list.
- **Four statuses:** `live | parked | concluded | dropped`. Simple, designed for inquiry.

## When to open a click

Open a click when a conversation or line of thought has enough substance to outlive the current session. Heuristics:

- Sean is exploring a design question across multiple turns
- You've tabled something with "let's come back to this"
- A decision is deferred pending more thought
- A line of inquiry has its own arc — opening question, accumulating sub-questions, eventual conclusion

Don't open a click for every exchange. One-off questions, quick clarifications, and routine operational work don't need one.

## Creating a click

    nsg click create --goal "How should we handle X?" [--parent-id <id>]

- The `--goal` is the question or inquiry — frame it as a question when possible.
- Use `--parent-id` to nest under an existing click (sub-question decomposition).
- The click starts in `live` status.
- Record the returned ID in the chat transcript so Sean can reference it later.

### Vocabulary discovery habit

When opening any new click, scan `docs/future/guild-vocabulary.md` for related terms and consider creating cross-links. The vocabulary tome holds latent metaphor concepts that imply future features; the only mechanism that surfaces them at the right moment is this manual habit.

## Viewing clicks

**Tree view** (primary orientation command):

    nsg click tree [--root-id <id>] [--status live] [--depth N]

Renders the click forest with box-drawing connectors and status indicators:
```
● How should the quest system evolve?                    [live]
  ├── ○ Is the friction removable or structural?         [concluded]
  ├── ● What should we call these things?                [live]
  └── ◇ Do quests need an event-log layer?               [parked]
```
Status indicators: `●` live, `◇` parked, `○` concluded, `✕` dropped.

**Show a single click** with links, parent, and children:

    nsg click show <id>

**List with filters:**

    nsg click list [--status live] [--status parked] [--limit 50]

**Extract a subtree** as a structured document (the continuity mechanism — one call to load a full subtree's context):

    nsg click extract --id <id> [--full] [--format md|json]

Without `--full`, shows goals only. With `--full`, includes conclusions.

## Status transitions

```
live ──> parked       (park — deliberately dormant, pick up later)
parked ──> live       (resume — back to active exploration)
live ──> concluded    (conclude — decision reached, conclusion required)
live ──> dropped      (drop — abandoned, reason required)
parked ──> concluded  (conclude directly from parked)
parked ──> dropped    (drop directly from parked)
```

Terminal states (concluded, dropped) are immutable — no further changes allowed.

**Park** a click (live -> parked):

    nsg click park --id <id>

**Resume** a parked click (parked -> live):

    nsg click resume --id <id>

**Conclude** a click with a decision:

    nsg click conclude --id <id> --conclusion "We decided to..."

**Drop** a click without a decision:

    nsg click drop --id <id> --conclusion "Moot because..."

## Sub-questions (children)

When a click spawns distinct sub-inquiries, create child clicks:

    nsg click create --goal "Sub-question A?" --parent-id <parent-id>

Children are the decomposition mechanism. Resolving a parent typically means resolving or dropping its children. But there's no automatic cascading — a parent can be concluded while children are still live (e.g., "we answered the main question; remaining sub-questions are moot").

## Links

Typed links connect clicks to each other or to writs (cross-substrate):

    nsg click link --source-id <click-id> --target-id <target-id> --link-type <type>

Link types:
- `related` — lateral cross-reference
- `commissioned` — this click's conclusion produced a commission (target is a writ ID)
- `supersedes` — this click replaces the target
- `depends-on` — this click can't be concluded until the target is resolved

Remove a link:

    nsg click unlink --source-id <id> --target-id <id> --link-type <type>

## Reparenting

Move a click to a new parent or to root:

    nsg click reparent --id <id> --parent-id <new-parent-id>
    nsg click reparent --id <id>                               # move to root

Circular parentage is detected and rejected.

## Short ID support

All ID parameters accept short prefixes (e.g., `c-mo1ee` instead of the full ID). The Ratchet resolves prefixes and errors on ambiguity.

## Clicks and session continuity

Clicks provide continuity across sessions. The flow is:

- **Startup** — scan the click tree with `nsg click tree --status live --status parked` to see what's in flight. Load details on demand with `nsg click show <id>`. Don't eagerly read every click.
- **During the session** — open new clicks for substantial inquiries; create child clicks for sub-questions. Conclude or drop clicks as decisions are reached.
- **Wrap-up** — park any live clicks that won't be continued immediately. Conclude clicks where decisions were reached this session. The click tree itself is the session's durable record of what was explored and decided.

Because click goals are immutable and conclusions are write-once, there's no body to maintain or keep in sync. The session transcript (joinable via session ID) holds the full exploration context. Clicks are just the structural skeleton — questions asked and answers given.

{
  "id": "w-mo0zt6qy-fdef4932e40f",
  "type": "brief",
  "status": "completed",
  "title": "Click CLI commands — Ratchet Package 2",
  "body": "# Click CLI commands — Ratchet Package 2

## Summary

Add `nsg click` CLI commands for the Ratchet apparatus. The Ratchet plugin (P1, landed as `v0.1.198`) provides the data layer and API — this commission adds the CLI surface so agents and humans can interact with clicks from the command line.

## Architecture Reference

- **Ratchet apparatus spec:** `docs/architecture/apparatus/ratchet.md` — CLI commands section defines all commands, parameters, and behavior.
- **Ratchet implementation (P1):** `packages/plugins/ratchet/src/` — the `RatchetApi` that CLI commands call into. The plugin already registers MCP tools (`src/tools/`); this commission adds the CLI layer that maps `nsg click-*` commands to those tools.
- **Clerk CLI as pattern:** Clerk's CLI commands (`packages/framework/cli/src/commands/`) show the established pattern for mapping CLI commands to plugin tool calls.

## Scope

CLI commands only. No Oculus changes, no migration, no changes to the Ratchet plugin itself.

### Delivers

**Core commands:**

1. `nsg click-create --goal "..." [--parent-id <id>]` — create a click
2. `nsg click-show <id>` — show a single click with links and children summary (positional ID argument)
3. `nsg click-list [--status <status>] [--root-id <id>] [--limit N]` — list clicks with filters. `--status` should be repeatable for multi-value filter.
4. `nsg click-tree [--root-id <id>] [--status <status>] [--depth N]` — render the click tree with status indicators. This is the primary orientation command. Output format:
   ```
   ● How should the quest system evolve?                    [live]
     ├── ○ Is the friction removable or structural?         [concluded]
     ├── ● What should we call these things?                [live]
     └── ◇ Do quests need an event-log layer?               [parked]
   ```
   Status indicators: `●` live, `◇` parked, `○` concluded, `✕` dropped.
5. `nsg click-extract --id <id> [--full] [--format md|json]` — render a subtree as a structured document. Default format: markdown. `--full` includes conclusions; without it, goals only. This is the continuity mechanism — one call to load a full subtree's context.

**Lifecycle commands:**

6. `nsg click-park --id <id>` — live → parked
7. `nsg click-resume --id <id>` — parked → live
8. `nsg click-conclude --id <id> --conclusion "..."` — live|parked → concluded
9. `nsg click-drop --id <id> --conclusion "..."` — live|parked → dropped

**Link and structure commands:**

10. `nsg click-link --source-id <id> --target-id <id> --link-type <type>` — add a typed link (related, commissioned, supersedes, depends-on). Target can be a click ID or a writ ID (cross-substrate).
11. `nsg click-unlink --source-id <id> --target-id <id> --link-type <type>` — remove a link
12. `nsg click-reparent --id <id> [--parent-id <id>]` — move a click to a new parent. When `--parent-id` is omitted, the click becomes a root node.

### Short ID support

All `--id`, `--parent-id`, `--root-id`, `--source-id`, and `--target-id` parameters should accept short ID prefixes (e.g., `c-mo0xpq` instead of the full ID). Resolution is handled by `RatchetApi.resolveId()` which is already implemented in P1. Error on ambiguity with a clear message listing matches.

### Positional ID on show

`click-show` should accept the ID as a positional first argument (no flag required): `nsg click-show c-mo0xpq`. The named `--id` flag should also work as an alternative.

### Does NOT deliver

- Oculus visualization (Package 3)
- Migration from quest writs (Package 4)
- `click-commission` sugar command (deferred — requires coordination with Clerk's commission-post)
- Changes to the Ratchet plugin/API

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All 12 commands implemented and registered in the CLI
- [ ] `click-tree` renders a readable tree with status indicators and correct nesting
- [ ] `click-extract` produces structured markdown (default) and JSON output
- [ ] `click-extract --full` includes conclusions; without `--full`, goals only
- [ ] Short ID prefix accepted on all ID parameters; ambiguity produces a clear error
- [ ] `click-show` accepts positional ID argument
- [ ] `--status` is repeatable on `click-list` and `click-tree` for multi-value filter
- [ ] `click-reparent` without `--parent-id` makes the click a root node
- [ ] `click-conclude` and `click-drop` reject missing `--conclusion`
- [ ] All commands produce clear error messages for invalid operations (wrong status, not found, etc.)",

  "body": "# Click CLI commands — Ratchet Package 2

## Summary

Add `nsg click` CLI commands for the Ratchet apparatus. The Ratchet plugin (P1, landed as `v0.1.198`) provides the data layer and API — this commission adds the CLI surface so agents and humans can interact with clicks from the command line.

## Architecture Reference

- **Ratchet apparatus spec:** `docs/architecture/apparatus/ratchet.md` — CLI commands section defines all commands, parameters, and behavior.
- **Ratchet implementation (P1):** `packages/plugins/ratchet/src/` — the `RatchetApi` that CLI commands call into. The plugin already registers MCP tools (`src/tools/`); this commission adds the CLI layer that maps `nsg click-*` commands to those tools.
- **Clerk CLI as pattern:** Clerk's CLI commands (`packages/framework/cli/src/commands/`) show the established pattern for mapping CLI commands to plugin tool calls.

## Scope

CLI commands only. No Oculus changes, no migration, no changes to the Ratchet plugin itself.

### Delivers

**Core commands:**

1. `nsg click-create --goal "..." [--parent-id <id>]` — create a click
2. `nsg click-show <id>` — show a single click with links and children summary (positional ID argument)
3. `nsg click-list [--status <status>] [--root-id <id>] [--limit N]` — list clicks with filters. `--status` should be repeatable for multi-value filter.
4. `nsg click-tree [--root-id <id>] [--status <status>] [--depth N]` — render the click tree with status indicators. This is the primary orientation command. Output format:
   ```
   ● How should the quest system evolve?                    [live]
     ├── ○ Is the friction removable or structural?         [concluded]
     ├── ● What should we call these things?                [live]
     └── ◇ Do quests need an event-log layer?               [parked]
   ```
   Status indicators: `●` live, `◇` parked, `○` concluded, `✕` dropped.
5. `nsg click-extract --id <id> [--full] [--format md|json]` — render a subtree as a structured document. Default format: markdown. `--full` includes conclusions; without it, goals only. This is the continuity mechanism — one call to load a full subtree's context.

**Lifecycle commands:**

6. `nsg click-park --id <id>` — live → parked
7. `nsg click-resume --id <id>` — parked → live
8. `nsg click-conclude --id <id> --conclusion "..."` — live|parked → concluded
9. `nsg click-drop --id <id> --conclusion "..."` — live|parked → dropped

**Link and structure commands:**

10. `nsg click-link --source-id <id> --target-id <id> --link-type <type>` — add a typed link (related, commissioned, supersedes, depends-on). Target can be a click ID or a writ ID (cross-substrate).
11. `nsg click-unlink --source-id <id> --target-id <id> --link-type <type>` — remove a link
12. `nsg click-reparent --id <id> [--parent-id <id>]` — move a click to a new parent. When `--parent-id` is omitted, the click becomes a root node.

### Short ID support

All `--id`, `--parent-id`, `--root-id`, `--source-id`, and `--target-id` parameters should accept short ID prefixes (e.g., `c-mo0xpq` instead of the full ID). Resolution is handled by `RatchetApi.resolveId()` which is already implemented in P1. Error on ambiguity with a clear message listing matches.

### Positional ID on show

`click-show` should accept the ID as a positional first argument (no flag required): `nsg click-show c-mo0xpq`. The named `--id` flag should also work as an alternative.

### Does NOT deliver

- Oculus visualization (Package 3)
- Migration from quest writs (Package 4)
- `click-commission` sugar command (deferred — requires coordination with Clerk's commission-post)
- Changes to the Ratchet plugin/API

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All 12 commands implemented and registered in the CLI
- [ ] `click-tree` renders a readable tree with status indicators and correct nesting
- [ ] `click-extract` produces structured markdown (default) and JSON output
- [ ] `click-extract --full` includes conclusions; without `--full`, goals only
- [ ] Short ID prefix accepted on all ID parameters; ambiguity produces a clear error
- [ ] `click-show` accepts positional ID argument
- [ ] `--status` is repeatable on `click-list` and `click-tree` for multi-value filter
- [ ] `click-reparent` without `--parent-id` makes the click a root node
- [ ] `click-conclude` and `click-drop` reject missing `--conclusion`
- [ ] All commands produce clear error messages for invalid operations (wrong status, not found, etc.)",

540	    item: "Continued operational triage after the spider fix landed. (1) Discovered that the one 'running' engine at the time of investigation (inventory-check on rig-mnrpc8xr, from 2026-04-09) was a zombie — status=running in the books but no corresponding babysitter subprocess anywhere in the process tree. Its parent writ was already cancelled. Attempted nsg rig cancel, which failed because the cancel path refuses to operate when the writ is already terminal. Reaped the rig via direct sqlite edit of books_spider_rigs (rig→cancelled, running engine→cancelled, pending engines→cancelled, completedAt set). (2) Bumped maxConcurrentEngines from default 3 to 5 in /workspace/vibers/guild.json (sanctum-adjacent config in the guild workspace). (3) Sean restarted the daemon; new spider code + throttle both took effect, five rigs spawned and filled the new throttle cap. (4) Dispatched three commissions for the rig-lifecycle bugs discovered during this episode: brief w-mnt2p847 for zombie engine detection and reaping, brief w-mnt2pbuc for writ-cancel cascading to rig-cancel, mandate w-mnt2pfr2 (complexity 2) for allowing nsg rig cancel to operate on terminal writs. (5) Sean noticed rig-mnt2escc (one of the two freshly-spawned reverted writs) had been stuck on its review engine for 16+ minutes with no progress. Investigation found the review engine had status=running, startedAt, but no sessionId and no corresponding animator session record — a brand new zombie, born after the daemon restart. Cancelled via nsg rig cancel (which worked cleanly this time, including cascading the writ to cancelled). Reposted the writ as w-mnt35eoi (complexity 3) via commission.sh. (6) Root-caused the stranded engine: the old daemon died between the spider's 'patch engine to running' write and the 'patch sessionId on design.run() return' write, leaving the engine in an inconsistent state the new daemon's tryRun() doesn't recover from (it only picks up pending engines). Sean rejected the naive startup-sweep fix as too risky for non-quick (clockwork) engines, so opened quest w-mnt3di0m-22b4a5dac36a to explore three approach families (restart-time reaping with per-category policy, atomic running→sessionId transition, graceful daemon shutdown on SIGINT/SIGTERM in foreground mode). Linked the quest as related to the zombie brief. Quest file committed in /workspace/vibers. This log entry bundles a guild.json commit in the sanctum."

558	    item: "Astrolabe efficiency quest (w-mnt3t5h8) — profile + two experimental commissions dispatched. Sean asked for the top-3 self-contained quests ready to dispatch; recommended astrolabe profiling as #2 alongside race-safe pending session recovery (w-mnszh7kz) and daemon-restart stranding (w-mnt3di0m). Initially thought profiling needed more instrumentation, but Sean corrected: guild books already carry session records with metadata.role, metadata.engineId, metadata.writId. Guild is not set up to run research commissions on itself, so Sean asked Coco to handle the profile directly. Pulled 368 sessions via nsg session show, filtered to 51 astrolabe.sage sessions (22 reader, 17 analyst, 12 spec-writer). Built per-engine rollup: reader is 65% of astrolabe spend ($69.75 of $107.35), mean $3.17/session, max $8.22, 5.2× spread. Critical finding from cacheRead/cacheWrite ratios: reader takes ~25 turns on average, re-reading its ~107k cache each turn. Cost is dominated by turn count NOT load size (cheapest reader: 66k/24 turns/$1.59; most expensive: 118k/26 turns/$8.22). Conversation-cache-sharing downstream saves only 27% (much less than expected), suggesting reader and analyst are partially redundant. One-off observability finding: a reader session silently degraded when its output tools weren't available and still billed $2.26 — flagged for follow-up quest. Drafted profile in .scratch/ then moved to experiments/data/2026-04-10-astrolabe-profile.md with full intervention ranking (A. single-shot reader 10-20× savings; B. merge reader/analyst ~50% savings; C. shrink per-turn cache-read; D. fail-fast tool-availability; E. not pursued). Updated quest file in vibers with findings and new Next Steps (committed as Coco in vibers 8f263e8). Drafted two commission briefs in .scratch/ for experimental secondary rig templates: brief-astrolabe-ssr-template.md (planning-ssr + brief-ssr writ type, single-shot reader prompt) and brief-astrolabe-mra-template.md (planning-mra + brief-mra writ type, merged reader-analyst stage). Both briefs constrain the autonomous agent to leave the production planning template untouched, minimize diff to astrolabe.ts (concurrent commissions conflict surface), emit engineId metadata that keeps profiling comparable, and stop at the test-passing point (patron will dispatch real briefs through the new types separately). Dispatched both via bin/commission.sh --codex nexus --complexity 5 as w-mnt966d8-f28cc1ed1632 (SSR) and w-mnt968x4-4d76fdb2342f (MRA). This sanctum commit bundles the profile artifact, both brief drafts, the commission-log updates the dispatches produced, and the coco-log entry."

570	    item: "Code review of the claude-code session provider that escalated into a protocol-level spec-and-gaps analysis and then narrowed back once the diagnostic data came in. Started from Sean's question of whether the provider warranted surgical cleanup, spec-equivalent rewrite, or clean-start rebuild. Read index.ts, detached.ts, babysitter.ts, mcp-server.ts end to end and produced a numbered review: launchAttached dead code contradicting its own docstring; fire-and-forget pre-write of pending SessionDocs racing the tool server's authorize callback; pollForProcessInfo spinning on SessionDoc for a PID the babysitter already knows; duplicated SSE server boilerplate across mcp-server.ts and babysitter.ts; 'fetch failed' substring hack in retry logic; leaked system-prompt temp dir; parser writing to process.stderr as a side effect; isEntryPoint brittleness; exit code dropping signal info on SIGTERM. Recommended spec-equivalent rewrite of the lifecycle protocol over surgery or clean-start, on the grounds that the architecture was right but contracts were implicit. Discovered that afd173b had landed an orphan recovery (recoverOrphans in animator startup.ts) — a mechanism I had almost recommended building from scratch — but with three gaps: scans running-only not pending, uses claude PID instead of babysitter PID, runs only at guild startup. Those three gaps exactly mapped to the zombie-session symptoms Sean had been seeing. Drafted a clean-room spec.md in .scratch/detached-sessions/ defining the SessionDoc state machine (pending/running/terminal), contracts between guild and session host, reconciliation rules, cancellation targeting process groups, authorization anchored on the session record, and a container extension path — deliberately avoiding SQLite, Zod, .nexus/dlq, specific ports, MCP/SSE, and attached mode in the protocol itself. Drafted gaps.md with 12 numbered gaps, a deliberate-omissions section, and an 8-phase migration plan mapped to 6 commissions. On Sean's challenge, investigated whether the MCP-tools regression Sean had reported was a real symptom of the provider split: initial hypothesis was z.toJSONSchema round-tripping corrupting schemas; a parallel session (a01667b9) advanced a prompt/naming hypothesis (bare 'spec-write' vs fully-qualified 'mcp__nexus-guild__spec-write'); transcript grep across 51 astrolabe sessions returned zero mcp__nexus-guild__* calls and animas saying 'tools aren't available,' ruling out both. Confirmed the bug lives upstream of the provider by adding a one-line stderr diagnostic at the top of launchDetached; Sean ran an astrolabe rig and the log said 'received 0 tools.' Walked the chain: astrolabe tool definitions (permission:'astrolabe:read'), loom role-qualification (registers astrolabe.sage correctly, dependency-scoped permission filtering passes), Instrumentarium matchesPermission() comparing grant.level === permission — for a grant astrolabe:read the comparison is 'read' === 'astrolabe:read' which never matches. Sanity-checked via grep across plugins: parlour, codexes, and instrumentarium's own test fixtures use bare-level form ('read', 'write'); clerk and astrolabe use plugin-qualified form. tool.ts:78-90 documents the contract as bare-level, so the broken plugins are violating a documented convention. At that point discovered the parallel session (a01667b9) had landed on the same bug hours earlier and was already dispatching a fix mandate. Updated gaps.md to downgrade G6 severity and rescope Phase 0 to the upstream chain walk (now effectively closed by the parallel session's mandate). Reverted the diagnostic patch. Removed an unrelated spammy console.log from arbor.ts's Guild.apparatus() lookup that Sean had noticed in the diagnostic output — the comment said 'defining apparatus method' but the function is the lookup, which fires on every apparatus() call during session launches. Pushed the arbor fix as 338bdd0 on origin/main — the one commit this entry covers. Also drafted a small cleanup-mandate brief for the orphan review items (stderr-parser side effect, retry substring hack, exit-code signal loss, isEntryPoint, dead import, docToProviderResult transcript-drop, extractFinalAssistantText semantic question) landing at .scratch/brief-claude-code-cleanup.md. Final recommendation on C2-C6 (the detached-sessions rewrite): park the rewrite, publish the clean-room spec as the canonical detached-sessions.md, archive gaps.md as an experiment artifact, commission only when a specific gap bites. Sean is reviewing that recommendation."

575	    item: "Iterated the detached-sessions clean-room spec and gaps doc toward a heartbeat-based reconciliation model. Starting point was the earlier draft's PID-based liveness check (reconciler calls process.kill(pid,0) against a host PID carried in the session record), which Sean flagged was both misaligned with the container-extension path and tangled up with the question of how the guild would know whether it had been down when computing silence windows. Walked through three design passes out loud. Pass 1 proposed a three-layer hybrid: tool calls as implicit heartbeats, explicit heartbeats to fill gaps, platform probes as fallback, plus guild downtime tracked via 'last clean shutdown' with ordering hooks into the shutdown path. Sean pushed back on all three axes simultaneously: reconciler location (is it guild or host?), whether platform probes are needed at all given host diversity, and proposed replacing clean-shutdown tracking with a running guild_alive_at timestamp on a 30-60s timer that handles crashes uniformly. Conceded all three — reconciler is a guild process; platform probes add no information that heartbeat silence doesn't already convey (a host that can't heartbeat is dead to the anima either way); guild self-heartbeat is simpler and more robust than shutdown tracking. Pass 2 proposed keeping implicit-tool-call heartbeats as an optimization on top of explicit heartbeats. Sean asked 'what's the benefit of the implicit tool usage heartbeat complexity?' — did the honest analysis and concluded 'probably not worth it': explicit heartbeats on a 30-60s timer are cheap enough that adding a write to tool authorization earns nothing, couples auth to liveness state, creates a subtle failure mode where auth succeeds but activity update fails, and adds no real freshness because the reconciler threshold is measured in multiples of the interval. Dropped implicit heartbeats. Final model: explicit heartbeat endpoint, host-side 30-60s timer, guild wall-clock timestamp on receipt (avoids clock skew), at-least-once delivery without DLQ (stale heartbeats have no replay value), guild_alive_at updated on its own timer, startup reconciler computes downtime_credit = now - previous_guild_alive_at and applies it to every non-terminal session's silence budget on the first pass only, reconciler rule is the one-liner 'if now - last_activity_at - downtime_credit > staleness_threshold, transition to failed' with threshold ~3-5x heartbeat interval. Rewrote spec.md's Reconciliation section end-to-end around this model; softened the 'process identifier' language in the ready-report contract to 'cancellation handle' (an opaque token meaningful only to the host's platform — pgid for local, container ID for container), reframed the Cancellation section with handle-kind dispatch so the container extension path needs no schema churn beyond adding a new kind, and rewrote the Extension: Container-Hosted Sessions section to explicitly state the heartbeat model is platform-agnostic and nothing changes across host types except the cancellation mechanism. Updated the Failure Modes table rows for pending/running crashes and the guild-restart row to mention downtime credit. Rewrote gaps.md to match: G1 now calls out the absence of any heartbeat mechanism (not just the missing PID); G3 now includes 'no guild self-heartbeat' as a co-gap with the missing periodic reconciler; G4 is rewritten from 'wrong PID' to 'PID-based liveness is the wrong shape entirely — spec is heartbeat-based' with a fix-shape that deletes the process.kill call outright; G5 clarified that the cancel handle is opaque and tagged by kind. Rewrote Phase 1 of the migration plan to pre-write last_activity_at on spawn and drop the 'record host PID at pre-write' step (pending sessions will be caught by heartbeat timeout instead of PID probe). Rewrote Phase 2 from 'extend recoverOrphans to pending + periodic' into a full 8-step heartbeat-based-reconciliation plan: session-record schema (last_activity_at field), heartbeat endpoint, babysitter heartbeat timer, guild self-heartbeat with a dedicated single-row table or well-known key, downtime credit computation at startup, reconciler rewrite to the one-liner rule, periodic reconciler timer (unref'd, single-flight guarded), legacy-record fix-up policy (treat pre-spec records as fresh on first sight). Rewrote Phase 3 (cancellation) to use cancelHandle: {kind, ...} instead of the dual-PID cancelMetadata shape — structure is ready for container kinds without a second schema change. Updated the commission table C2/C3 descriptions to match. Spec and gaps remain in .scratch/detached-sessions/ pending Sean's decision on publishing spec.md as canonical docs/architecture/detached-sessions.md and archiving gaps.md as an experiment artifact. This session is a post-compaction continuation of 7ea4ca40-345c-4abb-bfb6-e8011522978a (the earlier entry for this session ID is the pre-compaction half)."

581	    item: "Diagnosed why the astrolabe planning pipeline has been silently degraded — and the cause was not what either Sean or Coco initially suspected. Sean opened with a hypothesis: maybe the friendly tool names in the sage.md instructions (plan-show, spec-write, ...) don't line up with what the anima actually sees, which in Claude Code would be mcp__nexus-guild__<tool>. Confirmed the naming scheme by reading packages/plugins/claude-code/src/index.ts + mcp-server.ts and cross-checking against real tool-use events in experiments/data/transcripts/. Then pulled 51 astrolabe anima sessions from books_animator_transcripts in /workspace/vibers/.nexus/nexus.db (22 reader, 17 analyst, 12 spec-writer) and enumerated every tool_use block — zero calls to any mcp__nexus-guild__* tool across the entire history. The animas themselves said it out loud in multiple sessions: 'the Astrolabe tools aren't available as deferred tools' → fallback to direct better-sqlite3 writes against the guild DB. Discarded the naming hypothesis. Ran a thorough review of all 60 plugin-contributed tools capturing name/permission/callableBy via a Python scanner, plus all role definitions (guild-level in vibers/guild.json: steward *:*, artificer tools:*, reviewer []; kit-level: only astrolabe.sage with astrolabe:read/astrolabe:write/clerk:read and strict:true). Found two incompatible permission-string conventions across plugins: bare-level form (animator, codexes, parlour, tools) vs plugin-qualified form (clerk, astrolabe, spider). Traced the bug to instrumentarium.ts matchesPermission() which compares grant.level === permission — for a tool declaring permission:'astrolabe:read' against a grant astrolabe:read, the comparison becomes 'read' === 'astrolabe:read' → false. Latent because every live role uses wildcards (steward/artificer/reviewer) except astrolabe.sage which is the only role exercising the non-wildcard qualified path in the whole system. Sage resolves to the empty tool set and has been doing so since astrolabe shipped. Answered Sean's direct question about session-record/session-running (bare 'write' is correct — tool.ts:78-90 documents bare-level as the convention; pluginId is already attached at registration time; no auto-prefix). Drafted mandate at .scratch/mandate-tool-permission-convention.md — Option 1 (normalize qualified-form tools to bare-level) rather than Option 2 (make matcher accept both forms). Iterated twice per Sean's requests: added an Instrumentarium test that codifies the convention (register a tool with permission:'myplugin:read', grant myplugin:read, confirm it does NOT resolve — proves the matcher only recognizes bare-level), and added an explicit 'Affected plugins' section up top enumerating clerk (11 tools), astrolabe (7), spider (8), plus a hands-off list of plugins that must not be touched (animator, codexes, parlour, tools, loom, oculus). Also added a registration-time guard to throw if a tool declares a permission containing a colon. Tried to post via bin/commission.sh — Arbor startup logging leaked into stdout and broke JSON parsing inside the script; surfaced the error and stopped per standing orders on CLI breakage. Sean posted the commission manually. Notable ethnographic finding: multiple historical sage sessions worked around the missing tools by opening better-sqlite3 from node_modules and writing directly to the guild DB — silent tool-resolution failures producing silent-but-wrong behavior rather than loud crashes."

Findings written to experiments/data/2026-04-15-astrolabe-cache-reuse-analysis.md: cross-session shared-conv inheritance is weak (~27%, replicating the profile), MRA's merged-session structure delivers ~42% cache_write reduction on the clean brief, and the 'share a role prompt for prefix caching' rationale underlying baseline's three-stage pipeline is not supported by the data. Based on that finding, drafted a comprehensive promotion writ at .scratch/brief-astrolabe-mra-promotion.md covering five deliverables: rename rig templates to two-phase-planning (default) and three-phase-planning (preserved non-default), split the sage role into four job-specific roles with standalone instruction files, shrink engine prompts to thin plan-id shims, change the default rigTemplateMapping, and remove conversationId session-reuse givens from three-phase-planning stages. Quest body updated with the analysis summary and the detailed promotion plan."

  - session: 7ea4ca40-345c-4abb-bfb6-e8011522978a
    date: 2026-04-13
    item: "Code review of the claude-code session provider that escalated into a protocol-level spec-and-gaps analysis and then narrowed back once the diagnostic data came in. Started from Sean's question of whether the provider warranted surgical cleanup, spec-equivalent rewrite, or clean-start rebuild. Read index.ts, detached.ts, babysitter.ts, mcp-server.ts end to end and produced a numbered review: launchAttached dead code contradicting its own docstring; fire-and-forget pre-write of pending SessionDocs racing the tool server's authorize callback; pollForProcessInfo spinning on SessionDoc for a PID the babysitter already knows; duplicated SSE server boilerplate across mcp-server.ts and babysitter.ts; 'fetch failed' substring hack in retry logic; leaked system-prompt temp dir; parser writing to process.stderr as a side effect; isEntryPoint brittleness; exit code dropping signal info on SIGTERM. Recommended spec-equivalent rewrite of the lifecycle protocol over surgery or clean-start, on the grounds that the architecture was right but contracts were implicit. Discovered that afd173b had landed an orphan recovery (recoverOrphans in animator startup.ts) — a mechanism I had almost recommended building from scratch — but with three gaps: scans running-only not pending, uses claude PID instead of babysitter PID, runs only at guild startup. Those three gaps exactly mapped to the zombie-session symptoms Sean had been seeing. Drafted a clean-room spec.md in .scratch/detached-sessions/ defining the SessionDoc state machine (pending/running/terminal), contracts between guild and session host, reconciliation rules, cancellation targeting process groups, authorization anchored on the session record, and a container extension path — deliberately avoiding SQLite, Zod, .nexus/dlq, specific ports, MCP/SSE, and attached mode in the protocol itself. Drafted gaps.md with 12 numbered gaps, a deliberate-omissions section, and an 8-phase migration plan mapped to 6 commissions. On Sean's challenge, investigated whether the MCP-tools regression Sean had reported was a real symptom of the provider split: initial hypothesis was z.toJSONSchema round-tripping corrupting schemas; a parallel session (a01667b9) advanced a prompt/naming hypothesis (bare 'spec-write' vs fully-qualified 'mcp__nexus-guild__spec-write'); transcript grep across 51 astrolabe sessions returned zero mcp__nexus-guild__* calls and animas saying 'tools aren't available,' ruling out both. Confirmed the bug lives upstream of the provider by adding a one-line stderr diagnostic at the top of launchDetached; Sean ran an astrolabe rig and the log said 'received 0 tools.' Walked the chain: astrolabe tool definitions (permission:'astrolabe:read'), loom role-qualification (registers astrolabe.sage correctly, dependency-scoped permission filtering passes), Instrumentarium matchesPermission() comparing grant.level === permission — for a grant astrolabe:read the comparison is 'read' === 'astrolabe:read' which never matches. Sanity-checked via grep across plugins: parlour, codexes, and instrumentarium's own test fixtures use bare-level form ('read', 'write'); clerk and astrolabe use plugin-qualified form. tool.ts:78-90 documents the contract as bare-level, so the broken plugins are violating a documented convention. At that point discovered the parallel session (a01667b9) had landed on the same bug hours earlier and was already dispatching a fix mandate. Updated gaps.md to downgrade G6 severity and rescope Phase 0 to the upstream chain walk (now effectively closed by the parallel session's mandate). Reverted the diagnostic patch. Removed an unrelated spammy console.log from arbor.ts's Guild.apparatus() lookup that Sean had noticed in the diagnostic output — the comment said 'defining apparatus method' but the function is the lookup, which fires on every apparatus() call during session launches. Pushed the arbor fix as 338bdd0 on origin/main — the one commit this entry covers. Also drafted a small cleanup-mandate brief for the orphan review items (stderr-parser side effect, retry substring hack, exit-code signal loss, isEntryPoint, dead import, docToProviderResult transcript-drop, extractFinalAssistantText semantic question) landing at .scratch/brief-claude-code-cleanup.md. Final recommendation on C2-C6 (the detached-sessions rewrite): park the rewrite, publish the clean-room spec as the canonical detached-sessions.md, archive gaps.md as an experiment artifact, commission only when a specific gap bites. Sean is reviewing that recommendation."
    commissionable: true

  - session: 7ea4ca40-345c-4abb-bfb6-e8011522978a
    date: 2026-04-13
    item: "Iterated the detached-sessions clean-room spec and gaps doc toward a heartbeat-based reconciliation model. Starting point was the earlier draft's PID-based liveness check (reconciler calls process.kill(pid,0) against a host PID carried in the session record), which Sean flagged was both misaligned with the container-extension path and tangled up with the question of how the guild would know whether it had been down when computing silence windows. Walked through three design passes out loud. Pass 1 proposed a three-layer hybrid: tool calls as implicit heartbeats, explicit heartbeats to fill gaps, platform probes as fallback, plus guild downtime tracked via 'last clean shutdown' with ordering hooks into the shutdown path. Sean pushed back on all three axes simultaneously: reconciler location (is it guild or host?), whether platform probes are needed at all given host diversity, and proposed replacing clean-shutdown tracking with a running guild_alive_at timestamp on a 30-60s timer that handles crashes uniformly. Conceded all three — reconciler is a guild process; platform probes add no information that heartbeat silence doesn't already convey (a host that can't heartbeat is dead to the anima either way); guild self-heartbeat is simpler and more robust than shutdown tracking. Pass 2 proposed keeping implicit-tool-call heartbeats as an optimization on top of explicit heartbeats. Sean asked 'what's the benefit of the implicit tool usage heartbeat complexity?' — did the honest analysis and concluded 'probably not worth it': explicit heartbeats on a 30-60s timer are cheap enough that adding a write to tool authorization earns nothing, couples auth to liveness state, creates a subtle failure mode where auth succeeds but activity update fails, and adds no real freshness because the reconciler threshold is measured in multiples of the interval. Dropped implicit heartbeats. Final model: explicit heartbeat endpoint, host-side 30-60s timer, guild wall-clock timestamp on receipt (avoids clock skew), at-least-once delivery without DLQ (stale heartbeats have no replay value), guild_alive_at updated on its own timer, startup reconciler computes downtime_credit = now - previous_guild_alive_at and applies it to every non-terminal session's silence budget on the first pass only, reconciler rule is the one-liner 'if now - last_activity_at - downtime_credit > staleness_threshold, transition to failed' with threshold ~3-5x heartbeat interval. Rewrote spec.md's Reconciliation section end-to-end around this model; softened the 'process identifier' language in the ready-report contract to 'cancellation handle' (an opaque token meaningful only to the host's platform — pgid for local, container ID for container), reframed the Cancellation section with handle-kind dispatch so the container extension path needs no schema churn beyond adding a new kind, and rewrote the Extension: Container-Hosted Sessions section to explicitly state the heartbeat model is platform-agnostic and nothing changes across host types except the cancellation mechanism. Updated the Failure Modes table rows for pending/running crashes and the guild-restart row to mention downtime credit. Rewrote gaps.md to match: G1 now calls out the absence of any heartbeat mechanism (not just the missing PID); G3 now includes 'no guild self-heartbeat' as a co-gap with the missing periodic reconciler; G4 is rewritten from 'wrong PID' to 'PID-based liveness is the wrong shape entirely — spec is heartbeat-based' with a fix-shape that deletes the process.kill call outright; G5 clarified that the cancel handle is opaque and tagged by kind. Rewrote Phase 1 of the migration plan to pre-write last_activity_at on spawn and drop the 'record host PID at pre-write' step (pending sessions will be caught by heartbeat timeout instead of PID probe). Rewrote Phase 2 from 'extend recoverOrphans to pending + periodic' into a full 8-step heartbeat-based-reconciliation plan: session-record schema (last_activity_at field), heartbeat endpoint, babysitter heartbeat timer, guild self-heartbeat with a dedicated single-row table or well-known key, downtime credit computation at startup, reconciler rewrite to the one-liner rule, periodic reconciler timer (unref'd, single-flight guarded), legacy-record fix-up policy (treat pre-spec records as fresh on first sight). Rewrote Phase 3 (cancellation) to use cancelHandle: {kind, ...} instead of the dual-PID cancelMetadata shape — structure is ready for container kinds without a second schema change. Updated the commission table C2/C3 descriptions to match. Spec and gaps remain in .scratch/detached-sessions/ pending Sean's decision on publishing spec.md as canonical docs/architecture/detached-sessions.md and archiving gaps.md as an experiment artifact. This session is a post-compaction continuation of 7ea4ca40-345c-4abb-bfb6-e8011522978a (the earlier entry for this session ID is the pre-compaction half)."
    commissionable: false
    justification: sanctum

  - session: a01667b9-74d2-4e36-82d3-0d0cc6557636
    date: 2026-04-13
    item: "Diagnosed why the astrolabe planning pipeline has been silently degraded — and the cause was not what either Sean or Coco initially suspected. Sean opened with a hypothesis: maybe the friendly tool names in the sage.md instructions (plan-show, spec-write, ...) don't line up with what the anima actually sees, which in Claude Code would be mcp__nexus-guild__<tool>. Confirmed the naming scheme by reading packages/plugins/claude-code/src/index.ts + mcp-server.ts and cross-checking against real tool-use events in experiments/data/transcripts/. Then pulled 51 astrolabe anima sessions from books_animator_transcripts in /workspace/vibers/.nexus/nexus.db (22 reader, 17 analyst, 12 spec-writer) and enumerated every tool_use block — zero calls to any mcp__nexus-guild__* tool across the entire history. The animas themselves said it out loud in multiple sessions: 'the Astrolabe tools aren't available as deferred tools' → fallback to direct better-sqlite3 writes against the guild DB. Discarded the naming hypothesis. Ran a thorough review of all 60 plugin-contributed tools capturing name/permission/callableBy via a Python scanner, plus all role definitions (guild-level in vibers/guild.json: steward *:*, artificer tools:*, reviewer []; kit-level: only astrolabe.sage with astrolabe:read/astrolabe:write/clerk:read and strict:true). Found two incompatible permission-string conventions across plugins: bare-level form (animator, codexes, parlour, tools) vs plugin-qualified form (clerk, astrolabe, spider). Traced the bug to instrumentarium.ts matchesPermission() which compares grant.level === permission — for a tool declaring permission:'astrolabe:read' against a grant astrolabe:read, the comparison becomes 'read' === 'astrolabe:read' → false. Latent because every live role uses wildcards (steward/artificer/reviewer) except astrolabe.sage which is the only role exercising the non-wildcard qualified path in the whole system. Sage resolves to the empty tool set and has been doing so since astrolabe shipped. Answered Sean's direct question about session-record/session-running (bare 'write' is correct — tool.ts:78-90 documents bare-level as the convention; pluginId is already attached at registration time; no auto-prefix). Drafted mandate at .scratch/mandate-tool-permission-convention.md — Option 1 (normalize qualified-form tools to bare-level) rather than Option 2 (make matcher accept both forms). Iterated twice per Sean's requests: added an Instrumentarium test that codifies the convention (register a tool with permission:'myplugin:read', grant myplugin:read, confirm it does NOT resolve — proves the matcher only recognizes bare-level), and added an explicit 'Affected plugins' section up top enumerating clerk (11 tools), astrolabe (7), spider (8), plus a hands-off list of plugins that must not be touched (animator, codexes, parlour, tools, loom, oculus). Also added a registration-time guard to throw if a tool declares a permission containing a colon. Tried to post via bin/commission.sh — Arbor startup logging leaked into stdout and broke JSON parsing inside the script; surfaced the error and stopped per standing orders on CLI breakage. Sean posted the commission manually. Notable ethnographic finding: multiple historical sage sessions worked around the missing tools by opening better-sqlite3 from node_modules and writing directly to the guild DB — silent tool-resolution failures producing silent-but-wrong behavior rather than loud crashes."
    commissionable: false
    justification: sanctum

Good. Now — the skill is committed but the agent file updates are staged for your review. Here's what needs to change in `coco.md` (and the system prompt that mirrors it):

**Changes needed:**

1. **Startup § orientation** — replace `nsg writ list --type quest --status ready --status active --status waiting --limit 100` with `nsg click tree --status live --status parked` (or `nsg click list --status live --status parked --limit 100` until the tree root-detection bug is fixed)

2. **Capturing Todos** (§ Collaborating on Documents) — replace "store it as a `quest`" with "store it as a click"

3. **Quests section** — rewrite entirely for clicks:
   - Replace the quest startup command
   - Replace `nsg writ show <id>` with `nsg click show <id>`
   - Replace "invoke the quests skill" with "invoke the clicks skill (`.claude/skills/clicks/SKILL.md`)"
   - The vocabulary discovery habit stays the same (just s/quest/click/)

4. **Wrap-up skill** — update the reference from "quests" to "clicks" (line 14: "Session-to-session continuity is now carried by **clicks**...")

The click skill itself is already committed and ready. Want me to go ahead and draft those agent file changes in `.scratch/` so you can review them side-by-side, or would you rather I just describe the diffs and you make the edits?
