# Quest → Click Migration: Closed/Cancelled Quests

Assessment of all 37 completed/cancelled/failed quests for residual value worth preserving as concluded clicks.

Legend:
- ✅ = completed, clear conclusion reached
- ❌ = cancelled, explicit reason recorded
- 💀 = dissolved by construction (a structural change made the question moot)

---

## Worth Preserving as Concluded Clicks

These closed quests contain decisions or conclusions that future sessions should be able to discover. They should become concluded clicks with the conclusion captured.

### Decisions That Inform Active Work

| Quest | Status | Conclusion | Why Preserve |
|-------|--------|------------|--------------|
| **w-mnsx90p1** Writ status model simplification | ✅ | Shipped: 6-value model (new/open/stuck/completed/failed/cancelled). ready/active/waiting removed. | Active reference — agents need to know the canonical status set |
| **w-mnsww5kg** Plugin-contributed writ types | ✅ | Shipped: kit `writTypes` contribution field, config-overrides-kit policy | Referenced by multiple active quests (multi-rig, rig templates) |
| **w-mnt0jin1** File-canonical quest bodies | ✅ | Shipped: convention layer, body lives in file, row has stub+Goal | Historical — dissolved several sibling quests; documents the convention that the click model now replaces |
| **w-mnsx3onr** Rename 'quest' | ✅ | Resolved: renamed to 'click', shipped as Ratchet apparatus | Direct ancestor of current click model |
| **w-mo0gias9** Pilgrimage: alternative quest storage | ✅ | Concluded: explored Ratchet/click as separate apparatus, adopted | Direct ancestor of current click model |
| **w-mnszh7kz** Race-safe pending session recovery | ✅ | Shipped: age-gated + sweep-ordered pending session reaping in Animator | Active reference — the fix is in production, agents working on Animator should know about it |

### Architectural Decisions (Shipped Features)

| Quest | Status | Conclusion | Why Preserve |
|-------|--------|------------|--------------|
| **w-mnsiduuj** Detached sessions architecture | ✅ | Shipped: full detached session architecture | Major architectural decision; children capture review findings and test plan |
| **w-mnsidw40** Detached sessions commission review | ✅ | Code quality strong; identified integration gaps (tool server boot, session registration) | Review findings are reference material |
| **w-mnsidxaz** Detached sessions smoke test plan | ✅ | Test plan executed and passed | Test methodology reference |
| **w-mnsidp6m** Startup lifecycle phases | ✅ | Shipped: unified kit wiring, startup phases | Active reference — the startup model agents build on |
| **w-mnsidnza** Patron input block type | ✅ | Shipped: `patron-input` block type for structured patron input | Active feature; agents building on input requests need this |
| **w-mnsierwe** Patron input early spec | ✅ | Early design that led to the shipped patron-input block type | Historical precursor |
| **w-mnsidmrt** Engine blocking on external conditions | ✅ | Shipped: block types, checkers, rig-resume | Foundation for patron-input and future blocking |
| **w-mnsidlkp** Block checker failure signal | ✅ | Shipped: checkers can signal permanent failure | Complements engine blocking |
| **w-mnsidkdk** Kit-contributed roles | ✅ | Shipped: roles as kit contributions | Framework feature reference |
| **w-mnsidj6m** Configurable rig templates | ✅ | Shipped: guild.json rig template configuration | Active feature reference |
| **w-mnsidhyd** Normalize ID formats | ✅ | Shipped: `{prefix}-{base36ts}{hex}` convention | Active convention agents must follow |
| **w-mnsieqo6** Kit-contributed rig templates | ✅ | Shipped: kits can contribute rig templates | Framework feature reference |
| **w-mnsiepgz** Astrolabe sage instructions | ✅ | Shipped: sage role instructions for astrolabe pipeline | Active reference |
| **w-mnsicty7** Spider engine throttling | ✅ | Shipped: per-rig and global engine concurrency limits | Active feature reference |
| **w-mnsidd2u** Babysitter MCP handshake race | ✅ | Shipped: fix for MCP tool-list race at session start | Bug fix reference |
| **w-mnsidbuq** Oculus Spider page enhancements | ✅ | Shipped: rig template display, rig list improvements | UI feature |
| **w-mnsidfjk** Oculus feedback page | ✅ | Shipped: visual patron feedback UI | UI feature |
| **w-mnsidebi** Decision analysis → QuestionSpec tags | ✅ | Shipped: analyst metadata flows to feedback UI tags | Integration feature |
| **w-mnsidgr1** QuestionSpec tags + feedback filters | ✅ | Shipped: tags field on questions, UI filter toggles | UI feature |
| **w-mnsifdm1** Oculus web dashboard brief | ✅ | Shipped: full Oculus apparatus | Major feature |
| **w-mnsifesq** Oculus web dashboard spec | ✅ | Shipped: detailed spec for Oculus | Spec reference |

---

## Not Worth Preserving (Dissolved / Cancelled with Clear Reason)

These quests were either dissolved by construction or cancelled with the reasoning fully captured in the quest body itself. The conclusions are self-evident or no longer relevant.

### Dissolved by File-Canonical Quest Bodies (w-mnt0jin1)
All three dissolved when quest bodies moved to files — the problems they described (concurrent writes, edit history, editing ergonomics) vanished because the substrate changed:

| Quest | Status | Why Not Preserve |
|-------|--------|-----------------|
| **w-mnszkcd5** Quest body edit history | 💀 | Git log on quest files = free audit trail. Problem dissolved. |
| **w-mnszjvrr** Quest body editing ergonomics | 💀 | Files editable with native tools. Problem dissolved. |
| **w-mnswwgah** Concurrent session writes | 💀 | Git conflict semantics apply. Problem dissolved. |

### Cancelled — Premature or Superseded

| Quest | Status | Why Not Preserve |
|-------|--------|-----------------|
| **w-mo0ffh3c** Quest workflow and orientation rituals | ❌ | Premature umbrella, cancelled same day. One-child umbrellas don't earn their keep. |
| **w-mnt106rv** Quest-helper CLI wrapper | ❌ | Superseded by click CLI (Ratchet P2). |
| **w-mnszhkjq** Unify capability registries | ❌ | Speculative refactor without forcing function. Idea noted, no decision needed. |
| **w-mnszhjq5** --setting-sources user research | ❌ | Superseded by containerization strategy. |
| **w-mnszhc65** Codex boundaries & permissions | ❌ | Superseded by containerization — filesystem isolation makes this moot. |
| **w-mnsyzpni** Verification contracts (Nyquist) | ❌ | Parked idea capture. The pattern is noted in the explicit-contracts quest cluster. |
| **w-mnsi25h6** Smoke test: opt-in dispatch | ❌ | Test passed. Pure operational artifact, no decision value. |
| **w-mnshgkja** Quest-scribe subagent | ❌ | Gating condition (context bloat) didn't trigger. Cancelled correctly. |

---

## Assessment Summary

| Category | Count | Recommendation |
|----------|-------|---------------|
| Decisions informing active work | 6 | Create concluded clicks with conclusions |
| Shipped architectural features | 22 | Create concluded clicks (batch) — these form a "decision digest" |
| Dissolved by construction | 3 | Drop — reasoning is self-evident |
| Cancelled / superseded | 8 | Drop — reasoning captured in quest bodies |
| **Total** | **37** | **28 worth preserving, 11 drop** |

### Value Assessment

**High value in preserving shipped-feature conclusions as concluded clicks:**
The 28 "worth preserving" quests document architectural decisions and shipped features that agents need to know about. Currently this knowledge exists only in quest bodies (which are about to be retired) and scattered commit history. Migrating them as concluded clicks creates a queryable decision record — directly addressing the "concluded clicks as forgotten knowledge" concern raised in click `c-mo1itn3x`.

**Low value in the cancelled/dissolved quests:**
The 11 "drop" quests have their reasoning fully captured in the quest body text. If we need to reference why something was cancelled, `nsg writ show` still works — the quest substrate isn't being deleted, just retired from active use.

### Recommendation

For the 28 worth preserving: create them as pre-concluded clicks (status: `concluded`, conclusion: one-line summary of what shipped/was decided). This is a batch operation that could be scripted. The question is whether this bulk backfill is worth the effort now vs. creating concluded clicks on-demand when a future session needs to reference one.

lastPrompt: I think we should got a bit deeper, and review the goal+context+notes+etc for a quest, and decompose it into 1-or-many clicks, depending on the questions raised/answered with them

content.text:
Done! Two documents in `.scratch/`:

**`.scratch/quest-migration-active.md`** — the 51 open quests decomposed into a click tree:
- **56 proposed clicks** from 43 migratable quests
- **8 quests superseded** by the click model itself (quest CLI wrapper, event-log layer, re-parent, filter, tree index, quest MVP substrate, and more)
- Organized by cluster: Unlocking Autonomous Operation (27 clicks from 15 quests), Astrolabe Agility (7 from 6), Click Implementation (3 live from 7, 4 superseded), Quest System Improvements (1 live from 5, 4 superseded), Containerized Sessions (2 from 2), and 16 standalone root clicks

**`.scratch/quest-migration-closed.md`** — the 37 closed/cancelled quests assessed for residual value:
- **28 worth preserving** as concluded clicks (6 decisions informing active work + 22 shipped architectural features)
- **9 safe to drop** (3 dissolved by construction, 8 cancelled with clear reasoning)
- The big insight: the 22 shipped-feature quests form a natural "decision digest" that would directly address the concern in click `c-mo1itn3x` about concluded knowledge being forgotten

The key question for you, Sean: do you want to bulk-backfill those 28 concluded clicks, or create them on-demand when future sessions actually need the reference? The on-demand approach is cheaper but risks the "forgotten knowledge" problem we already identified.

message.content: the 'everything is a question' feels a bit rigid.. i wonder if there is room in clicks for a more imperative voice in some/many/all cases? ('research XXX' vs 'how can XXX'?) Or does forcing the questions lead to better outcomes, even though the list looks kinda weird. genuinely interested in reasoned thoughts here vs blind agreement

content.text:
Good question, and one I actually have a position on rather than just going along with the design as stated.

**The question form was me being overly mechanical in the decomposition, not a property the click model actually requires.** The click model says goals are immutable and conclusions are write-once — it doesn't mandate interrogative grammar. I defaulted to questions because "decision node" implies "question to answer," but that's a narrower reading than the model needs.

Here's where I actually land after thinking through the cases:

**The real boundary isn't grammatical mood — it's reasoning vs. obligation.**

Clicks track *inquiry*: things you're trying to figure out, decide, evaluate, research. Writs track *obligation*: things you've committed to deliver. That distinction holds regardless of whether the click goal says "How should X work?" or "Evaluate X" or "Research X."

Consider some concrete pairs from the decomposition:

| Forced question | More natural form | Lost anything? |
|---|---|---|
| "How should the Clockworks MVP timer apparatus work?" | "Design the Clockworks MVP timer apparatus" | No — conclusion still captures the design decision |
| "What open issues exist with X013 quality scorers?" | "Track X013 quality scorer open issues" | No — it's a tracking umbrella either way |
| "How do we build end-to-end daemon integration tests?" | "Scope daemon integration test coverage" | No — and the imperative is actually *more precise* about what the click is for |

But then there are clicks where the question form genuinely earns its keep:

| Question form | Why it works |
|---|---|
| "Why does the reviewer rarely reject?" | The question *is* the inquiry — you can't rephrase this as an imperative without losing the specificity |
| "Should brief → mandate collapse under multi-rig?" | Binary decision — the question frames the conclusion space |
| "Discard-and-recommission vs reconcile-engine vs stuck-escalation — which policy?" | The alternatives are in the question; the conclusion picks one |

**The pattern I see:** Questions are strong when the click is genuinely a *decision* with nameable alternatives or a *mystery* with a specific unknown. Questions are awkward ceremony when the click is a *research activity*, a *design exercise*, or an *umbrella/tracker*.

**The slippery slope concern:** If click goals can be imperative, do they blur with writs? I don't think so, as long as the semantic test is "inquiry vs. obligation." "Research prompt-injection surfaces" is an inquiry even though it's imperative. "Implement prompt-injection defenses" is an obligation. The verb carries the distinction, not the punctuation.

**My recommendation:** Allow both voices. The guidance in the skill should say something like: *"Name what you're trying to learn, decide, or evaluate — not what you're trying to build. Questions are natural for decisions and mysteries; imperative voice is natural for research, design exercises, and tracking umbrellas. Either way, the conclusion should capture what was figured out."*

Want me to revise the decomposition trees with more natural goal phrasing? The "wall of How should..." would read much better as a mix of voices.

message.content: yes, revise teh decomposition tree with natural goal phrasing. then let me know if you think there is value in migrating the concluded/cancelled ones... assuming we solve teh 'lost concluded knowledge' gap by some sort of review/synthesis of closed clicks it could be useful

---

## 🌳 Standalone Root Clicks

These root quests each represent a single inquiry and map 1:1 to clicks:

```
├── Design Clockworks MVP timer apparatus
├── Scope daemon end-to-end integration test coverage
├── Diagnose MCP tool-server 500s dropping mid-session on astrolabe reader
├── Design engine-declared MCP tool preconditions with pre-session verification
├── Design session consult — entering an existing autonomous session interactively
├── Should engine completion be predicated on tool-call trace rather than session exit?
├── Design recovery from daemon restarts that strand engines in running state
├── Track X013 quality scorer open issues (umbrella)
│   ├── Design unified instrument context for cache efficiency
│   └── Design structured concern lists on quality scorers (QS-2)
├── Rethink commission-log granularity when briefs spawn multiple mandates
├── Codify the explicit-contracts principle — make implicit shared understanding into upfront artifacts
├── Evaluate work-tracking primitives beyond writs+clicks (backlog, seeds, threads)
├── Evaluate explicit design contracts as first-class planning artifacts (beyond UI)
├── Adopt assumptions mode as the default interaction style for design conversations
└── Design prompt-injection hardening for commission-sourced prompts
```
→ 16 clicks from 16 quests

**Notes:**
- "Diagnose MCP 500s" and "Should engine completion be predicated on..." are the two that stay as questions — one is a genuine mystery, the other is a genuine yes/no decision
- "Rethink commission-log granularity" uses natural deliberative voice — it's neither a pure question nor a design directive
- The explicit-contracts pair (principle + planning-artifact application) remain distinct clicks
- "Adopt assumptions mode" is imperative because the quest body already has a strong directional hypothesis — the click is about validating and codifying it, not deciding whether to do it

# Quest → Click Migration: Active Quests

Proposed click decomposition for all 51 open (new/open) quests. Each quest becomes one or more clicks based on the distinct questions/inquiries embedded within it. Indentation shows parent→child structure.

Goals use natural voice — questions for genuine decisions and mysteries, imperative/declarative for research activities, design exercises, and tracking umbrellas.

Legend: `→ N clicks` = number of clicks proposed from this quest

---

## 🌳 Unlocking Autonomous Operation
*Quest: w-mo0e2m9q — umbrella*

```
Unlock autonomous hopper-based operation for Nexus
├── Redesign Astrolabe planner for intent-and-constraints specs with tiered decision model
│   ├── Design the preference model that graduates patron corrections into planning defaults
│   ├── Design tiered decision model that proceeds on best-guess defaults
│   └── Design task session handoff notes for the implement-loop engine
│       ├── Where should handoff notes be stored — task writ, session record, or dedicated book?
│       └── Should all previous handoff notes be queryable, or just the immediately prior one?
├── Choose recovery policy for seal-engine rebase conflicts
│   └── Discard-and-recommission vs reconcile-engine vs stuck-escalation — which?
├── Why does the reviewer rarely reject?
│   └── Is the reviewer too weak, or are specs tight enough that pass-through is legitimate?
├── Design concurrency-control substrate for parallel planning rigs
│   ├── Design OCC validation of plans against observed read sets
│   ├── Resolve plan-vs-plan conflicts when two Astrolabe sessions overlap
│   ├── Design intake tier model (backlog / ready pool / parked) to decouple dump from dispatch
│   └── Design load-bearing blocked_by links as scheduler prerequisites
├── Replace narrative-blob specs with structured task decomposition and acceptance verification
│   ├── Produce prompt guidance for verify-command quality
│   └── Design multi-turn conversational analyst mode for plan refinement
├── Separate writs (obligations) from rigs (attempts) — the multi-rig refactor
│   ├── Untangle the three meanings overloaded onto parent-child relationships
│   ├── Design cross-rig data flow and workspace persistence
│   ├── Design rig template generation beyond 'one template per writ type'
│   └── Should brief → mandate collapse into a single writ under multi-rig?
├── Design background monitoring of in-flight commissions (Vigil)
├── Design first-class internal commission flow (Petition)
└── Design cost tracking and token budget allocation (Coinmaster/Purse/Tithe)
```
→ 27 clicks from 15 quests

**Notes:**
- The umbrella quest itself becomes the root click — imperative voice fits naturally for a strategic objective
- w-mo0v636y (Decision-centric planner) decomposes into design sub-clicks plus child quest w-mo0znvsz (handoff notes) which has 2 genuine open questions
- w-mo0e31ca (Concurrency control) has 4 distinct design problems embedded in its goal
- w-mnsx8cz2 (Multi-rig refactor) umbrella + 4 children → 5 clicks; the brief→mandate collapse is a genuine yes/no decision so stays as a question
- "Why does the reviewer rarely reject?" stays as a question — it's a mystery, not a design task
- Vigil, Petition, Coinmaster are each single design clicks

---

## 🌳 Astrolabe Agility
*Quest: w-mo0v5wpc — umbrella*

```
Cut Astrolabe per-commission time and cost for autonomous batch operation
├── Design GSD-intel-style atlas as pre-built interpretive context for Astrolabe
│   ├── Design Layer 1: prose preamble from LLM mapper with brief-type → subset mapping
│   ├── Design Layer 2: deterministic repository facts from non-LLM tooling
│   └── Design Layer 3: staleness detection, refresh policy, atlas-as-engine-precondition
└── Profile and cut reader stage full-inventory cost
    └── Where do Astrolabe's time and tokens actually go across stages?
```
→ 7 clicks from 6 quests

**Notes:**
- Root and atlas umbrella are strategic objectives — imperative voice
- Layer children are design exercises
- Reader efficiency starts with a profiling question (genuine unknown) that feeds into interventions

---

## 🌳 Click Implementation
*Quest: w-mo0xpqwr — umbrella*

```
Implement the click model end-to-end [MOSTLY DONE — conclude after migration]
├── Design Coco's scope-closure discipline: explore/decide modes + context checkpoints
├── Design top-level click orientation as a focusing tool
│   ├── CLI: filter for top-level writs directly [SUPERSEDED by click tree]
│   ├── CLI: re-parent writs in any status [SUPERSEDED by click reparent]
│   └── CLI: generate quest tree index artifact [SUPERSEDED by click tree]
└── Design event-log layer for quest writs [SUPERSEDED by click immutability]
```
→ 3 live clicks from 7 quests (4 superseded)

**Notes:**
- Scope-closure is a genuine open design exercise — how should explore/decide modes work in practice?
- The focusing-tool concept migrates but its CLI-prereq children are all superseded by Ratchet's native capabilities
- Event-log is moot — clicks are immutable, no events to track

---

## 🌳 Improvements to the Quest System
*Quest: w-mo0ffbff — umbrella*

```
[LARGELY SUPERSEDED — click model replaces quest system]
├── Quest substrate MVP (w-mnswvmj7) [SUPERSEDED]
│   └── Design first-class queryable decision records with ratification flow → MIGRATES as standalone click
└── Rename 'quest' [COMPLETED → renamed to 'click']
```
→ 1 live click from this cluster

**Notes:**
- w-mnswwzdv (Decisions & ratification) transcends the quest→click migration — the question of making decisions queryable records with attribution and ratification is genuinely open and applies to the click model too
- Everything else in this tree is either shipped or superseded

---

*Quest: w-mo0f2led — umbrella*

```
Run every anima session in its own container for isolation and parallel safety
└── Achieve deterministic session directory isolation — container vs flag hardening
```

│   ├── How should cross-rig data flow and workspace persistence work?
│   ├── How should rig templates be generated beyond 'one template per writ type'?
│   └── Should brief → mandate collapse into a single writ under multi-rig?
├── How should background monitoring of in-flight commissions work? (Vigil)
├── How should internally-originated commissions work? (Petition flow)
└── How should cost tracking and token budget allocation work? (Coinmaster/Purse/Tithe)
```
→ 27 clicks from 15 quests

**Notes:**
- The umbrella quest itself becomes the root click
- w-mo0v636y (Decision-centric planner) has two sub-questions plus one child quest (handoff notes w-mo0znvsz) which itself decomposes into 2 questions
- w-mo0e31ca (Concurrency control) has 4 distinct sub-questions embedded in its goal

├── How should Coco's scope-closure discipline work? (explore/decide modes + checkpoints)
├── How should top-level clicks serve as a focusing tool for orientation?

│   ├── Should the CLI support re-parenting writs in any status? [SUPERSEDED by click model]

## 🌳 Standalone Root Clicks

These root quests each represent a single open question and map 1:1 to clicks:

```
├── How should the Clockworks MVP timer apparatus work?

├── Why do MCP tool-server 500s drop mid-session on baseline astrolabe reader?

| Cluster | Quests | Proposed Clicks | Superseded |
|---------|--------|----------------|------------|
| Unlocking autonomous operation | 15 | 27 | 0 |
| Astrolabe agility | 6 | 7 | 0 |
| Click implementation | 7 | 3 | 4 |
| Quest system improvements | 5 | 1 | 4 |

| **Total** | **51** | **56** | **8** |

8 quests are superseded by the click model itself and should be concluded/dropped rather than migrated. The remaining 43 quests decompose into 56 clicks (some quests embed multiple distinct questions).

### Two clicks already exist in Ratchet:
- `c-mo1itggx` "How should Coco's click-based session continuity work end-to-end?"
- `c-mo1itn3x` (child) "How do we prevent concluded clicks from becoming forgotten knowledge?"

These should be incorporated as standalone roots in the final tree.

lastPrompt: yes, revise teh decomposition tree with natural goal phrasing. then let me know if you think there is value in migrating the concluded/cancelled ones... assuming we solve teh 'lost concluded knowledge…

content.text:
Now, on the concluded/cancelled quest migration question — here's my honest assessment:

**I don't think bulk-migrating the 28 closed quests as concluded clicks is worth it right now.** Here's why:

1. **The value isn't in the click record — it's in the synthesis.** A concluded click that says "Shipped: kit `writTypes` contribution field" is barely more useful than grepping writ titles. The value Sean identified — solving the "lost concluded knowledge" gap — requires *synthesized* knowledge, not 28 individual tombstones. "We have kit-contributed writ types, roles, rig templates, and block types — all following the same pattern: `ctx.kits('<field>')` at init, config-overrides-kit" is useful. Twenty-eight separate concluded clicks restating individual shipping records isn't.

2. **The quest substrate isn't going away.** `nsg writ show` still works. The quest rows with their full bodies (especially the completed ones with detailed conclusions) remain queryable. We're retiring quests as the *active tracking* mechanism, not deleting the data. If a future session needs to know how kit-contributed roles shipped, `nsg writ show w-mnsidkdk` still returns the answer.

3. **The right shape for this is the "decision digest" concept from click `c-mo1itn3x`.** Whatever mechanism we build to prevent concluded clicks from being forgotten knowledge — that's the mechanism that should also absorb the historical quest conclusions. Backfilling 28 concluded clicks now, before that mechanism exists, just moves the data from one invisible-at-startup store (quest writs) to another (concluded clicks that aren't scanned).

4. **The 6 "decisions informing active work" might be worth individual concluded clicks** — things like the writ status model (6 values), the ID format convention, the pending session recovery fix. These are live constraints that active work bumps into. But even here, the right moment to create them is when a session actually needs the reference — at which point a concluded click gets created with a precise conclusion tied to the live context.

**Bottom line:** Solve the concluded-knowledge-gap mechanism first (the click `c-mo1itn3x` question), then decide what historical knowledge feeds into it. The quest substrate preserves everything in the meantime.

message.content: when you migrate tehse, its more than just goals, right? you are setting conclusions based on the content of the original quests as well?

content.text:
Yes — each quest body has context, design sketches, open questions, decisions reached, references, and status notes. The migration would distill all of that into:

- **Goal** — the natural-voice phrasing from the decomposition tree
- **Conclusion** — synthesized from the quest body's actual content: decisions made, approaches chosen, open questions remaining, links to shipped code or dependent quests

For the completed quests especially, the conclusions are rich. Something like w-mnsww5kg (plugin-contributed writ types) wouldn't just get "Shipped" — it'd get a conclusion like:

> Shipped as kit `writTypes` contribution field via `ctx.kits('writTypes')`. Config overrides kit contributions silently; duplicate kit contributions warn and skip (first wins). See `clerk.ts` lines 132-166.

That's the actual decision substance compressed into a conclusion that a future session can act on without having to read the full quest body.

For the *active* quests, the migration is different — they become live clicks with no conclusion, but the goal carries the refined framing from the quest body's accumulated context, not just the original one-liner. The quest body's design sketches, open questions, and partial decisions become the *reason* for sub-clicks in the decomposition — that's where the "one quest → multiple clicks" splits come from.

So yeah, the migration is a genuine synthesis pass, not a title copy. That's actually what makes me reconsider my previous position slightly — the concluded clicks wouldn't just be tombstones if the conclusions carry real distilled knowledge. The question is whether doing that synthesis *now* as a batch is worth it, or whether the quest substrate preserves the raw material well enough to do it on-demand later.

input.description: Check if pilgrimage quest file exists

content.content:
# Pilgrimage: explore alternative storage for quests beyond writs

## Goal

Exploratory quest. Ask whether the writ substrate is still the right home for quests, given real friction in the Oculus UI, the CLI vocabulary, the status model, and the surrounding tooling that was built for obligations. The question isn't "were writs a bad choice" — the quest-as-writ insight was clever and inherited a lot of machinery for free. The question is whether the fit friction is **removable in place** (plugin-level UI/CLI/status fixes) or **structural** (the writ substrate's obligation semantics will always leak into the quest experience, and the right answer is a different substrate). Outcome is a written assessment and a recommended direction — **not a migration decision, and explicitly not any implementation work**.

This is a pilgrimage per the guild vocabulary tome: a commission undertaken for knowledge, not deliverables. The output is lore — a tome or design note — not code.

## Status

**Concluded** — assessment complete, recommendation is **migrate**. The friction is structural, not removable. Build the Ratchet apparatus (new framework plugin, peer to Clerk) owning a clicks book in Stacks. Clicks are atomic, immutable decision-nodes in a tree. The "click" model replaces heavy quest synthesis documents with small question-nodes; the tree structure is the primary value, not prose bodies. Full assessment published at `docs/archive/design-sessions/click-model-assessment.md`. Architecture spec at `/workspace/nexus/docs/architecture/apparatus/ratchet.md`. Implementation scoped as 5 work packages; P1 (Ratchet apparatus foundation) dispatching.

## Next Steps

1. **Catalog the observed frictions.** Known starting points from Sean's 2026-04-15 framing and Coco's own experience:
   - **Oculus UI** — designed for obligation lifecycles. A quest sitting "open" indefinitely reads as a stalled mandate, not a live area of inquiry. Status pills convey "hasn't moved in N days, should it be stuck?" rather than "standing thread."
   - **CLI vocabulary** — commands are `nsg writ list`, `nsg writ show`, `nsg writ edit`. Coco's mental model during quest work is "I'm looking at a quest," but every command is writ-shaped. Constant translation tax. The quest-helper wrapper quest (`w-mnt106rv`) is a direct symptom.
   - **Status types** — the six-value model (`new | open | stuck | completed | failed | cancelled`) from `w-mnsx90p1` is a good *obligation* lifecycle. For quests, three of the six fit awkwardly: `stuck` (what does "stuck on a quest" mean? there's no escalation channel), `failed` (what's a failed inquiry?), `completed` (inquiries *conclude*, but "completed" carries an obligation-closure connotation that "concluded with a verdict" doesn't quite capture). What quests probably want is something closer to `live | parked | concluded` — three states, not six, with "parked" as a first-class terminal-ish state.
   - **Vocabulary friction** — the rename quest (`w-mnsx3onr`) is the surface version of this same tension. "Quest" doesn't sit right as a word; it's not clear whether the word is wrong, the concept is wrong, or the word-concept fit is wrong.
   - **Body/row split ergonomics** — quest bodies live in files (`w-mnt0jin1` file-canonical quest bodies shipped) but the writ substrate still wants a row body, leading to the current "stub in row, real body in file" dance. Not a catastrophe, but it's a tell that the fit isn't native.
   - Add more as Coco and Sean notice them. The first step of this quest is simply to write down the list honestly, without filtering or pre-judging.

2. **For each friction, distinguish removable vs. structural.** Key question: can a plugin-level fix (extend the UI, wrap the CLI, allow per-writ-type status vocabulary) neutralize it, or is the friction downstream of the writ substrate's fundamental obligation model?

3. **Sketch alternative substrate shapes.** Non-exhaustive candidates:
   - **Separate `quests` table** with its own schema, cross-referenced to writs via ID. Native status model, native UI, native CLI. Loses: free tree, free CDC, free lifecycle, free cross-refs — everything the writ store gave us.
   - **Document-canonical** (notes/wiki shape) with a thin index table. Body is canonical already per `w-mnt0jin1`; maybe the row is the thin layer and the body is the primary. Extreme version of the file-canonical direction.
   - **Plugin-contributed writ-type customization** — keep quests as writs but allow the `quest` writ type to contribute its own status vocabulary, UI renderer, CLI verbs. Maximizes reuse, pushes plugin surface further. May already be partially possible via the kit-contribution pattern (`w-mnsww5kg`).
   - **Something else** — possibly a shared substrate with the "backlog/seeds/threads" primitives question from `w-mnsz2ku6`. If those primitives need a non-writ home anyway, quests might ride along on the same substrate.

4. **Weigh tradeoffs.** What does a migration actually cost? What does living with the friction actually cost? Consider whether a plugin-contributed approach can coexist with the writ store indefinitely (likely yes) or whether there's a tipping point where the friction compounds.

5. **Recommend a direction.** Options space:
   - **Absorb** — declare the friction acceptable, stop opening quests about it, move on.
   - **Patch in place** — fix the Oculus/CLI/status frictions one by one via plugin extensions, keep the substrate.
   - **Migrate** — stand up a different substrate for quests, migrate existing data, deprecate the `quest` writ type. (Expensive; probably only justified if the friction is genuinely structural.)
   - **Coexist** — let the new substrate (if one is built) live alongside the writ store; choose per use case. Likely the least-disruptive path if the substrate question also opens for the `w-mnsz2ku6` primitives.

6. **Explicit non-goal:** no implementation. The output of this quest is a written assessment — probably a design note committed to the sanctum or a tome-shaped document — not code, not migrations, not new tables. If a direction is chosen, *that* work becomes its own quest or commission.

## Context

### The original clever insight

See `w-mnswvmj7` (Initial implementation and MVP) for the original framing. The core insight: quests were originally imagined as a *new* substrate, separate from writs. The late move was to realize they could be a new *type* on the existing writ substrate — inheriting tree, CDC, lifecycle, cross-references, CLI, UI all for free, at the cost of a type-system addition. Unmapped-to-rig-template means inert-by-construction: quests don't dispatch, so they don't trigger execution. Cheap, fast, shipped in a few days.

That insight is still real and still load-bearing. V1 of quests would probably not exist today if the writ-type path hadn't been taken. This quest is not a repudiation of that decision — it's an honest reassessment after some months of use.

### What the friction feels like in practice

Coco's subjective experience, as of 2026-04-15 after ~5 days of serious quest usage:

- **The CLI tax is small but constant.** Every quest operation translates through `nsg writ ...`. The instinct to write `nsg quest ...` shows up in muscle memory and has to be corrected. The wrapper quest (`w-mnt106rv`) exists because this tax is real enough to be worth removing.
- **The Oculus view is effectively unused for quests.** Quests don't appear on the dashboards Sean looks at because the dashboards are built around obligation lifecycle. Quest work happens entirely in chat + filesystem + CLI, with the database serving only as the canonical store. This is a smell: the "writs get the Oculus for free" benefit of the insight didn't actually land for the quest subtype.
- **The status model feels wrong at the edges.** `completed` is the most frequent offender — closing an inquiry doesn't feel like completing an obligation, and the ritual of "set to completed" carries a task-done vibe that doesn't match. `stuck` is rarely right for quests; we use `open` for everything live, which collapses the model to effectively two states (`open`/`closed-of-some-kind`), defeating the six-value design intent.
- **Parent/child semantics are sometimes strained.** See `w-mnsx9lbz` (parent-child relationship semantics, under the writ-substrate root). Writs use parentId for decomposition (a sub-mandate is work toward a mandate); quests use it for both decomposition ("this is a sub-inquiry") and conceptual grouping (an umbrella). That overload is an old friction surfacing in a new place.

### What writs still give us for free

Honest accounting of what the current substrate does well:

- **Tree structure.** Parent/child is implemented, has CLI support (`--parent-id`), has UI visibility, has an index in the schema.
- **CDC (change data capture).** Every writ change flows through the Laboratory's event stream and shows up in commission-log observability. Quests piggyback on this for free.
- **Lifecycle states.** The status model is built, tested, and wired into tooling. Even if three of the six values fit awkwardly for quests, the infrastructure is there.
- **Cross-references.** Writs can link to other writs via typed edges (in flight). A separate substrate would need to reimplement or integrate this.
- **CLI coverage.** `writ list`, `writ show`, `writ edit`, `writ complete`, `commission-post` — all work on quests today. Migration would require porting each.
- **Guild-level visibility.** Quests live in the same books as mandates, so they show up in guild-level queries, backups, exports. A separate substrate would be a separate data island.

Any migration has to earn each of these back. The bar is not low.

### Relationship to adjacent quests

- **`w-mnsz2ku6` Work-tracking primitives beyond writs+quests** — asks a related but broader question: are there categories of work (backlog items, seeds, threads) that need primitives beyond writs+quests entirely? If the answer converges on "yes, and they need a non-writ substrate," this quest's answer about quest storage might ride along: both questions could be served by the same new substrate. Worth reading both quests together before starting either.
- **`w-mnsx3onr` Rename 'quest' — the word doesn't sit right** — the vocabulary surface of this same substrate question. If we migrate, vocabulary can be chosen fresh with the new shape in mind. If we don't migrate, the vocabulary question stands on its own merits. Sibling under the same umbrella; cross-linked but not folded.
- **`w-mnswvmj7` Initial implementation and MVP** — the substrate this quest interrogates. The MVP was the right MVP to ship; questioning it now is a different kind of work than questioning it pre-ship. Both can be true.
- **`w-mnsx9lbz` Parent-child relationship semantics** — adjacent: the parent/child overload problem shows up in quests especially, but it's a concern of the writ substrate in general. If parent/child gets disentangled at the substrate level, some of the quest-specific friction eases without a substrate swap.

### Vocabulary tome cross-reference

**Pilgrimage** (`docs/future/guild-vocabulary.md`, line 133): "a commission undertaken not for deliverables, but for knowledge. Send an artificer (or sage) to explore an unfamiliar codebase, library, or technology — not to build anything, but to return with lore. The lore is then available to the guild for future commissions. System mapping: exploratory spikes, research tasks, 'go figure out how this library works and write up what you learn.' The output is a tome or lore entry, not code."

This quest is shaped exactly like a pilgrimage: exploratory, knowledge-producing, output is lore not code. The vocabulary term has been latent in the tome — this is the first quest Coco has opened that clearly earns the pilgrimage framing. Worth considering whether pilgrimage should become a bookmark quest alongside Petition, Vigil, Coinmaster, etc. (It's currently latent; see the vocabulary tome's bookmark list.) toolUseResult.stdout:
# Pilgrimage: explore alternative storage for quests beyond writs

## Goal

Exploratory quest. Ask whether the writ substrate is still the right home for quests, given real friction in the Oculus UI, the CLI vocabulary, the status model, and the surrounding tooling that was built for obligations. The question isn't "were writs a bad choice" — the quest-as-writ insight was clever and inherited a lot of machinery for free. The question is whether the fit friction is **removable in place** (plugin-level UI/CLI/status fixes) or **structural** (the writ substrate's obligation semantics will always leak into the quest experience, and the right answer is a different substrate). Outcome is a written assessment and a recommended direction — **not a migration decision, and explicitly not any implementation work**.

This is a pilgrimage per the guild vocabulary tome: a commission undertaken for knowledge, not deliverables. The output is lore — a tome or design note — not code.

## Status

**Concluded** — assessment complete, recommendation is **migrate**. The friction is structural, not removable. Build the Ratchet apparatus (new framework plugin, peer to Clerk) owning a clicks book in Stacks. Clicks are atomic, immutable decision-nodes in a tree. The "click" model replaces heavy quest synthesis documents with small question-nodes; the tree structure is the primary value, not prose bodies. Full assessment published at `docs/archive/design-sessions/click-model-assessment.md`. Architecture spec at `/workspace/nexus/docs/architecture/apparatus/ratchet.md`. Implementation scoped as 5 work packages; P1 (Ratchet apparatus foundation) dispatching.

## Next Steps

1. **Catalog the observed frictions.** Known starting points from Sean's 2026-04-15 framing and Coco's own experience:
   - **Oculus UI** — designed for obligation lifecycles. A quest sitting "open" indefinitely reads as a stalled mandate, not a live area of inquiry. Status pills convey "hasn't moved in N days, should it be stuck?" rather than "standing thread."
   - **CLI vocabulary** — commands are `nsg writ list`, `nsg writ show`, `nsg writ edit`. Coco's mental model during quest work is "I'm looking at a quest," but every command is writ-shaped. Constant translation tax. The quest-helper wrapper quest (`w-mnt106rv`) is a direct symptom.
   - **Status types** — the six-value model (`new | open | stuck | completed | failed | cancelled`) from `w-mnsx90p1` is a good *obligation* lifecycle. For quests, three of the six fit awkwardly: `stuck` (what does "stuck on a quest" mean? there's no escalation channel), `failed` (what's a failed inquiry?), `completed` (inquiries *conclude*, but "completed" carries an obligation-closure connotation that "concluded with a verdict" doesn't quite capture). What quests probably want is something closer to `live | parked | concluded` — three states, not six, with "parked" as a first-class terminal-ish state.
   - **Vocabulary friction** — the rename quest (`w-mnsx3onr`) is the surface version of this same tension. "Quest" doesn't sit right as a word; it's not clear whether the word is wrong, the concept is wrong, or the word-concept fit is wrong.
   - **Body/row split ergonomics** — quest bodies live in files (`w-mnt0jin1` file-canonical quest bodies shipped) but the writ substrate still wants a row body, leading to the current "stub in row, real body in file" dance. Not a catastrophe, but it's a tell that the fit isn't native.
   - Add more as Coco and Sean notice them. The first step of this quest is simply to write down the list honestly, without filtering or pre-judging.

2. **For each friction, distinguish removable vs. structural.** Key question: can a plugin-level fix (extend the UI, wrap the CLI, allow per-writ-type status vocabulary) neutralize it, or is the friction downstream of the writ substrate's fundamental obligation model?

3. **Sketch alternative substrate shapes.** Non-exhaustive candidates:
   - **Separate `quests` table** with its own schema, cross-referenced to writs via ID. Native status model, native UI, native CLI. Loses: free tree, free CDC, free lifecycle, free cross-refs — everything the writ store gave us.
   - **Document-canonical** (notes/wiki shape) with a thin index table. Body is canonical already per `w-mnt0jin1`; maybe the row is the thin layer and the body is the primary. Extreme version of the file-canonical direction.
   - **Plugin-contributed writ-type customization** — keep quests as writs but allow the `quest` writ type to contribute its own status vocabulary, UI renderer, CLI verbs. Maximizes reuse, pushes plugin surface further. May already be partially possible via the kit-contribution pattern (`w-mnsww5kg`).
   - **Something else** — possibly a shared substrate with the "backlog/seeds/threads" primitives question from `w-mnsz2ku6`. If those primitives need a non-writ home anyway, quests might ride along on the same substrate.

4. **Weigh tradeoffs.** What does a migration actually cost? What does living with the friction actually cost? Consider whether a plugin-contributed approach can coexist with the writ store indefinitely (likely yes) or whether there's a tipping point where the friction compounds.

5. **Recommend a direction.** Options space:
   - **Absorb** — declare the friction acceptable, stop opening quests about it, move on.
   - **Patch in place** — fix the Oculus/CLI/status frictions one by one via plugin extensions, keep the substrate.
   - **Migrate** — stand up a different substrate for quests, migrate existing data, deprecate the `quest` writ type. (Expensive; probably only justified if the friction is genuinely structural.)
   - **Coexist** — let the new substrate (if one is built) live alongside the writ store; choose per use case. Likely the least-disruptive path if the substrate question also opens for the `w-mnsz2ku6` primitives.

6. **Explicit non-goal:** no implementation. The output of this quest is a written assessment — probably a design note committed to the sanctum or a tome-shaped document — not code, not migrations, not new tables. If a direction is chosen, *that* work becomes its own quest or commission.

## Context

### The original clever insight

See `w-mnswvmj7` (Initial implementation and MVP) for the original framing. The core insight: quests were originally imagined as a *new* substrate, separate from writs. The late move was to realize they could be a new *type* on the existing writ substrate — inheriting tree, CDC, lifecycle, cross-references, CLI, UI all for free, at the cost of a type-system addition. Unmapped-to-rig-template means inert-by-construction: quests don't dispatch, so they don't trigger execution. Cheap, fast, shipped in a few days.

That insight is still real and still load-bearing. V1 of quests would probably not exist today if the writ-type path hadn't been taken. This quest is not a repudiation of that decision — it's an honest reassessment after some months of use.

### What the friction feels like in practice

Coco's subjective experience, as of 2026-04-15 after ~5 days of serious quest usage:

- **The CLI tax is small but constant.** Every quest operation translates through `nsg writ ...`. The instinct to write `nsg quest ...` shows up in muscle memory and has to be corrected. The wrapper quest (`w-mnt106rv`) exists because this tax is real enough to be worth removing.
- **The Oculus view is effectively unused for quests.** Quests don't appear on the dashboards Sean looks at because the dashboards are built around obligation lifecycle. Quest work happens entirely in chat + filesystem + CLI, with the database serving only as the canonical store. This is a smell: the "writs get the Oculus for free" benefit of the insight didn't actually land for the quest subtype.
- **The status model feels wrong at the edges.** `completed` is the most frequent offender — closing an inquiry doesn't feel like completing an obligation, and the ritual of "set to completed" carries a task-done vibe that doesn't match. `stuck` is rarely right for quests; we use `open` for everything live, which collapses the model to effectively two states (`open`/`closed-of-some-kind`), defeating the six-value design intent.
- **Parent/child semantics are sometimes strained.** See `w-mnsx9lbz` (parent-child relationship semantics, under the writ-substrate root). Writs use parentId for decomposition (a sub-mandate is work toward a mandate); quests use it for both decomposition ("this is a sub-inquiry") and conceptual grouping (an umbrella). That overload is an old friction surfacing in a new place.

### What writs still give us for free

Honest accounting of what the current substrate does well:

- **Tree structure.** Parent/child is implemented, has CLI support (`--parent-id`), has UI visibility, has an index in the schema.
- **CDC (change data capture).** Every writ change flows through the Laboratory's event stream and shows up in commission-log observability. Quests piggyback on this for free.
- **Lifecycle states.** The status model is built, tested, and wired into tooling. Even if three of the six values fit awkwardly for quests, the infrastructure is there.
- **Cross-references.** Writs can link to other writs via typed edges (in flight). A separate substrate would need to reimplement or integrate this.
- **CLI coverage.** `writ list`, `writ show`, `writ edit`, `writ complete`, `commission-post` — all work on quests today. Migration would require porting each.
- **Guild-level visibility.** Quests live in the same books as mandates, so they show up in guild-level queries, backups, exports. A separate substrate would be a separate data island.

Any migration has to earn each of these back. The bar is not low.

### Relationship to adjacent quests

- **`w-mnsz2ku6` Work-tracking primitives beyond writs+quests** — asks a related but broader question: are there categories of work (backlog items, seeds, threads) that need primitives beyond writs+quests entirely? If the answer converges on "yes, and they need a non-writ substrate," this quest's answer about quest storage might ride along: both questions could be served by the same new substrate. Worth reading both quests together before starting either.
- **`w-mnsx3onr` Rename 'quest' — the word doesn't sit right** — the vocabulary surface of this same substrate question. If we migrate, vocabulary can be chosen fresh with the new shape in mind. If we don't migrate, the vocabulary question stands on its own merits. Sibling under the same umbrella; cross-linked but not folded.
- **`w-mnswvmj7` Initial implementation and MVP** — the substrate this quest interrogates. The MVP was the right MVP to ship; questioning it now is a different kind of work than questioning it pre-ship. Both can be true.
- **`w-mnsx9lbz` Parent-child relationship semantics** — adjacent: the parent/child overload problem shows up in quests especially, but it's a concern of the writ substrate in general. If parent/child gets disentangled at the substrate level, some of the quest-specific friction eases without a substrate swap.

### Vocabulary tome cross-reference

**Pilgrimage** (`docs/future/guild-vocabulary.md`, line 133): "a commission undertaken not for deliverables, but for knowledge. Send an artificer (or sage) to explore an unfamiliar codebase, library, or technology — not to build anything, but to return with lore. The lore is then available to the guild for future commissions. System mapping: exploratory spikes, research tasks, 'go figure out how this library works and write up what you learn.' The output is a tome or lore entry, not code."

This quest is shaped exactly like a pilgrimage: exploratory, knowledge-producing, output is lore not code. The vocabulary term has been latent in the tome — this is the first quest Coco has opened that clearly earns the pilgrimage framing. Worth considering whether pilgrimage should become a bookmark quest alongside Petition, Vigil, Coinmaster, etc. (It's currently latent; see the vocabulary tome's bookmark list.) Not this quest's job to promote it, but flagging the possibility.

## References

- Parent: `w-mo0ffbff` Improvements to the quest system.
- Related sibling (substrate MVP that this questions): `w-mnswvmj7` Initial implementation and MVP.
- Related sibling (vocabulary surface of the same tension): `w-mnsx3onr` Rename 'quest' — the word doesn't sit right.
- Related top-level (adjacent substrate question): `w-mnsz2ku6` Work-tracking primitives beyond writs+quests — backlog, seeds, threads. Worth reading together.
- Related (writ substrate): `w-mnsx9lbz` Parent-child relationship semantics — disentanglement may remove some of this quest's friction without a swap.
- Related (in-place fix): `w-mnt106rv` Quest-helper CLI wrapper — a "patch in place" candidate that addresses the CLI tax specifically.
- Vocabulary: **Pilgrimage** — `docs/future/guild-vocabulary.md:133`. This quest is the first clear pilgrimage on the board; candidate to promote the vocabulary term to a bookmark.
- Trigger conversation: Coco session `166741dd` (2026-04-15). Transcript at `experiments/data/transcripts/166741dd-3d2c-4997-a0e1-55f9e98eb5cc.jsonl`.

## Notes

- 2026-04-15: opened at Sean's direction. Sean's framing: "i thought writs were clever, but there is real friction in the Oculus UI, CLI, status types, etc." This quest exists to take that friction seriously and see how much is removable vs. structural.
- Explicitly not a decision quest. Outcome is a written assessment; any actual migration (if chosen) would be scoped as a separate follow-up.
- Framed as a pilgrimage per the guild vocabulary tome. This is the first time that vocabulary term has been load-bearing on a quest body, which is a nice data point for the "vocabulary tome earns its keep through manual cross-linking" discipline.

content.content:
# Rename 'quest' — the word doesn't sit right

## Goal

Pick a better word than "quest" for the writ type that captures lightweight, working-note inquiry. The current name carries heroic-journey connotations that overshoot the mundane reality of most entries. Candidates on the table: `sketch`, `jotting`, `scribble` (or a better fourth option).

## Status

**Concluded** — superseded by the Ratchet/click model. The answer is "click" — the record type in the new Ratchet apparatus. The vocabulary question dissolved when the substrate question was answered: quests are being replaced entirely by clicks (atomic decision-nodes in a tree), not renamed within the writ substrate. See pilgrimage assessment: `docs/archive/design-sessions/click-model-assessment.md`.

## Next Steps

Check whether `sketch`, `jotting`, or `scribble` already appear in a different sense in the framework source or the guild metaphor docs (would disqualify the colliding candidates). Then pick one and do the rename pass: writ type name, skill directory, Coco agent instructions, metaphor doc, `nsg` CLI help text. Rename cost is bounded — fresh mechanism, no external dependencies.

## Context

"Quest" was the working name when the writ-substrate design shipped, and it's been rubbing wrong since. What we have is closer to a **parked thought** or a **working note**: a place to put a line of inquiry down so it doesn't get lost between sessions. Most entries are mundane. Calling them "quests" makes every stray thought sound like it's going on an odyssey.

**Criteria for a good name:**

- Matches the lightweight, "working note" feel of most entries
- Doesn't overpromise on structure or seriousness
- Fits the guild vocabulary (horological / craftwork / scholarly register)
- Works as a verb: "let me sketch this out", "jot that down"
- Survives pluralization and composition: `sketches` directory, `nsg sketch list`, "the decisions sketch"

**Candidates:**

- **Sketch** — draft-quality capture. Evokes the artist's notebook: quick lines, unfinished, meant to be worked up later. Pairs with "finished" artifacts elsewhere. Coco's current lean: closest to the "draft-quality, meant to be developed" semantics; works cleanly as noun and verb; pairs with the guild's craft register.
- **Jotting** — brief, informal written note. Punchier, more casual, accurate for the 80% of entries that never grow beyond a paragraph. Slightly awkward as a verb in instructions ("jotting-list" feels off).
- **Scribble** — most casual and honest about provisionality. Risks self-deprecating the mechanism into irrelevance; might read as dismissive when an autonomous planning agent consumes them.
- **Other ("workbook / atelier" register):** leaf, marginalia, notation, musing, inkling…

## References

- Parent quest: `w-mnswvmj7-2112b86f710a`
- Rename surface area: `.claude/skills/quests/`, `.claude/agents/coco.md`, `packages/framework/cli/` (CLI help), `/workspace/nexus/docs/guild-metaphor.md`

## Notes

- Worth checking: does the framework or metaphor doc already use any candidate word in a different sense?
- 2026-04-10: opened as child of writ substrate quest.

content.text: Now let me write the migration script. This is 38 clicks to create and immediately conclude/drop with synthesized conclusions.

input.content:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

SESSION="bae3f417-1473-4b6c-a4e9-fcd9c6c8d384"
cd /workspace/vibers

create_and_conclude() {
  local goal="$1"
  local conclusion="$2"
  local id
  id=$(nsg click create --goal "$goal" --created-session-id "$SESSION" 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.id')
  nsg click conclude --id "$id" --conclusion "$conclusion" --resolved-session-id "$SESSION" 2>/dev/null | jq -r '"\(.status) \(.id) \(.goal[0:60])"'
}

create_and_drop() {
  local goal="$1"
  local conclusion="$2"
  local id
  id=$(nsg click create --goal "$goal" --created-session-id "$SESSION" 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.id')
  nsg click drop --id "$id" --conclusion "$conclusion" --resolved-session-id "$SESSION" 2>/dev/null | jq -r '"\(.status) \(.id) \(.goal[0:60])"'
}

echo "=== COMPLETED QUESTS → CONCLUDED CLICKS ==="

create_and_conclude \
  "Evaluate whether quest/writ friction is removable or structural (Pilgrimage)" \
  "Structural. Quests migrated to the Ratchet apparatus — a new framework plugin peer to Clerk, owning a clicks book. Clicks are atomic, immutable decision-nodes in a tree with 4-status lifecycle (live/parked/concluded/dropped). Assessment at docs/archive/design-sessions/click-model-assessment.md."

create_and_conclude \
  "Make quest body files canonical, with writ rows as index/cache" \
  "Shipped as convention layer — no framework changes. Quest bodies live at writs/quests/<id>.md in the guild repo; row body is a stub. Git provides audit trail, native tools provide ergonomics. Dissolved three sibling quests (edit history, editing ergonomics, concurrent writes). Now superseded by click model which has no body files."

create_and_conclude \
  "Fix stale pending session reaping in Animator startup without breaking cancelMetadata test" \
  "Shipped: age-gated + sweep-ordered pending session reaping. Pending sessions older than a threshold are reaped during Animator startup, with the running sweep executing before the pending sweep to avoid async-tick races."

create_and_conclude \
  "Simplify writ status vocabulary to honest values" \
  "Shipped: 6-value model (new/open/stuck/completed/failed/cancelled). ready/active/waiting removed via startup migration. stuck is non-terminal with bidirectional open↔stuck transitions. See WritStatus type in clerk/src/types.ts."

create_and_conclude \
  "Find a better word than 'quest' for lightweight inquiry records" \
  "Resolved: the answer is 'click' — atomic decision-nodes in the Ratchet apparatus. The vocabulary question dissolved when the substrate question was answered: quests replaced entirely by clicks, not renamed within the writ substrate."

create_and_conclude \
  "Give plugins a first-class way to contribute writ types" \
  "Shipped as kit writTypes contribution field via ctx.kits('writTypes'). Config overrides kit contributions silently; duplicate kit contributions warn and skip (first-writer-wins). See clerk.ts registerKitWritTypes()."

create_and_conclude \
  "Detailed specification for the Oculus web dashboard apparatus" \
  "Shipped. Oculus serves a web dashboard via Hono, consuming pages and routes from kit contributions. Auto-maps guild tools as REST endpoints. Shared chrome and base stylesheet for contributed pages."

create_and_conclude \
  "Commission the Oculus web dashboard apparatus" \
  "Shipped. Full Oculus apparatus landed — framework-level web dashboard extensible via kit contribution pattern (pages, routes). Any plugin can add UI pages and API endpoints."

create_and_conclude \
  "Early specification for structured patron input during engine execution" \
  "Shipped as precursor to the full patron-input block type. Design validated: structured questions with typed answers, blocking until patron completes review."

create_and_conclude \
  "Enable plugins to contribute rig templates alongside writ types and mappings" \
  "Shipped. Plugins contribute rig templates via kit contributions, same pattern as writ types and roles. Guild config can override kit-contributed templates."

create_and_conclude \
  "Write proper sage role instructions for the Astrolabe planning pipeline" \
  "Shipped. Sage instructions cover the reader→analyst→spec-writer conversation chain with role-specific guidance for each stage of the Astrolabe pipeline."

create_and_conclude \
  "Prove detached sessions work end-to-end and survive guild restart" \
  "Passed. Detached session survived guild restart, MCP server reconnection, and result recording. Integration fixes (tool server boot, session registration, whitelisting) were prerequisites that landed first."

create_and_conclude \
  "Review the detached sessions implementation quality" \
  "Code quality strong — clean structure, ~3000 lines of tests, good separation of concerns, thorough error handling with retry + DLQ. Identified integration gaps in tool server boot timing, session registration, and session-running/session-record event whitelisting."

create_and_conclude \
  "Design architecture for guild-restart-surviving anima sessions" \
  "Shipped. Sessions run as detached child processes with MCP server bridging. Babysitter process manages lifecycle and handles guild restarts via reconnection. Result recording uses DLQ for resilience."

create_and_conclude \
  "Fix startup ordering bugs and unify kit contribution discovery" \
  "Shipped. Two-phase startup (register then start) with unified kit wiring. Standalone kits (plugin.kit) and apparatus sidecars (plugin.apparatus.supportKit) consumed identically via ctx.kits(). Fixed duplicate-registration bugs (e.g. Oculus nav bar duplicates)."

create_and_conclude \
  "Build a block type for structured patron input that engines can block on" \
  "Shipped. patron-input block type with InputRequestDoc (typed questions, answer validation), CLI tools (input-request-list/show/answer/complete/reject), and Spider blocking infrastructure integration."

create_and_conclude \
  "Enable engines to block on external conditions they cannot resolve" \
  "Shipped. Block types, checkers, and rig-resume mechanism. Engines signal unresolvable conditions; Spider tracks blocked state; rig-resume unblocks and retries. Foundation for patron-input and future blocking patterns."

create_and_conclude \
  "Let block checkers signal permanent failure, not just not-yet-cleared" \
  "Shipped. BlockType.check() returns a richer signal indicating permanent unresolvability (patron rejection, cancelled dependency, deleted resource). Checker can fail the engine directly instead of requiring manual rig-resume."

create_and_conclude \
  "Enable plugins to bundle purpose-built roles alongside their components" \
  "Shipped. Roles contributed via kit contributions — same pattern as tools, engines, writ types. Plugins ship roles without requiring guild.json manual edits."

create_and_conclude \
  "Let guilds configure writ-type-to-rig-template mappings in guild config" \
  "Shipped. spider.rigTemplates and spider.rigTemplateMappings in guild.json. Different writ types get different pipelines. Unmapped types are inert to Spider (opt-in dispatch)."

create_and_conclude \
  "Standardize ID generation format across all apparatus" \
  "Shipped. All apparatus use {prefix}-{base36_timestamp}{hex_random} convention. Sortable by creation time, collision-resistant, prefix identifies record type (w- writs, ses- sessions, etc)."

create_and_conclude \
  "Add tags to QuestionSpec types and tag-based filtering to feedback UI" \
  "Shipped. Optional tags field on all QuestionSpec types. Feedback UI displays tags on questions and provides toggleable alphabetical tag filters."

create_and_conclude \
  "Build a visual Oculus page for patron input request review and response" \
  "Shipped. Spider-owned Oculus page with interactive decision cards and scope toggles. Dark, monospaced UI for focused patron review. Replaces CLI-only input-request workflow for the Astrolabe decision-review flow."

create_and_conclude \
  "Map Astrolabe Decision.analysis metadata to QuestionSpec tags on InputRequestDocs" \
  "Shipped. Decision-review clockwork engine maps analyst classification metadata to ChoiceQuestionSpec tags, connecting structured analysis to the feedback UI's tag filtering."

create_and_conclude \
  "Fix planner sessions launching with zero MCP tools due to handshake race" \
  "Shipped. Fixed race condition where deferred_tools_delta at startup advertised only Claude Code built-ins and zero nexus-guild MCP tools. Root cause was babysitter MCP connection timing relative to tools/list."

  "Enhance Oculus Spider page with rig template display and rig list improvements" \

#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

SESSION="bae3f417-1473-4b6c-a4e9-fcd9c6c8d384"
cd /workspace/vibers

create_and_conclude() {
  local goal="$1"
  local conclusion="$2"
  local id
  id=$(nsg click create --goal "$goal" --created-session-id "$SESSION" 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.id')
  nsg click conclude --id "$id" --conclusion "$conclusion" --resolved-session-id "$SESSION" 2>/dev/null | jq -r '"\(.status) \(.id) \(.goal[0:60])"'
}

create_and_drop() {
  local goal="$1"
  local conclusion="$2"
  local id
  id=$(nsg click create --goal "$goal" --created-session-id "$SESSION" 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.id')
  nsg click drop --id "$id" --conclusion "$conclusion" --resolved-session-id "$SESSION" 2>/dev/null | jq -r '"\(.status) \(.id) \(.goal[0:60])"'
}

echo "=== COMPLETED QUESTS → CONCLUDED CLICKS ==="

create_and_conclude \
  "Evaluate whether quest/writ friction is removable or structural (Pilgrimage)" \
  "Structural. Quests migrated to the Ratchet apparatus — a new framework plugin peer to Clerk, owning a clicks book. Clicks are atomic, immutable decision-nodes in a tree with 4-status lifecycle (live/parked/concluded/dropped). Assessment at docs/archive/design-sessions/click-model-assessment.md."

create_and_conclude \
  "Make quest body files canonical, with writ rows as index/cache" \
  "Shipped as convention layer — no framework changes. Quest bodies live at writs/quests/<id>.md in the guild repo; row body is a stub. Git provides audit trail, native tools provide ergonomics. Dissolved three sibling quests (edit history, editing ergonomics, concurrent writes). Now superseded by click model which has no body files."

create_and_conclude \
  "Fix stale pending session reaping in Animator startup without breaking cancelMetadata test" \
  "Shipped: age-gated + sweep-ordered pending session reaping. Pending sessions older than a threshold are reaped during Animator startup, with the running sweep executing before the pending sweep to avoid async-tick races."

create_and_conclude \
  "Simplify writ status vocabulary to honest values" \
  "Shipped: 6-value model (new/open/stuck/completed/failed/cancelled). ready/active/waiting removed via startup migration. stuck is non-terminal with bidirectional open↔stuck transitions. See WritStatus type in clerk/src/types.ts."

create_and_conclude \
  "Find a better word than 'quest' for lightweight inquiry records" \
  "Resolved: the answer is 'click' — atomic decision-nodes in the Ratchet apparatus. The vocabulary question dissolved when the substrate question was answered: quests replaced entirely by clicks, not renamed within the writ substrate."

create_and_conclude \
  "Give plugins a first-class way to contribute writ types" \
  "Shipped as kit writTypes contribution field via ctx.kits('writTypes'). Config overrides kit contributions silently; duplicate kit contributions warn and skip (first-writer-wins). See clerk.ts registerKitWritTypes()."

create_and_conclude \
  "Detailed specification for the Oculus web dashboard apparatus" \
  "Shipped. Oculus serves a web dashboard via Hono, consuming pages and routes from kit contributions. Auto-maps guild tools as REST endpoints. Shared chrome and base stylesheet for contributed pages."

create_and_conclude \
  "Commission the Oculus web dashboard apparatus" \
  "Shipped. Full Oculus apparatus landed — framework-level web dashboard extensible via kit contribution pattern (pages, routes). Any plugin can add UI pages and API endpoints."

create_and_conclude \
  "Early specification for structured patron input during engine execution" \
  "Shipped as precursor to the full patron-input block type. Design validated: structured questions with typed answers, blocking until patron completes review."

create_and_conclude \
  "Enable plugins to contribute rig templates alongside writ types and mappings" \
  "Shipped. Plugins contribute rig templates via kit contributions, same pattern as writ types and roles. Guild config can override kit-contributed templates."

create_and_conclude \
  "Write proper sage role instructions for the Astrolabe planning pipeline" \
  "Shipped. Sage instructions cover the reader→analyst→spec-writer conversation chain with role-specific guidance for each stage of the Astrolabe pipeline."

create_and_conclude \
  "Prove detached sessions work end-to-end and survive guild restart" \
  "Passed. Detached session survived guild restart, MCP server reconnection, and result recording. Integration fixes (tool server boot, session registration, whitelisting) were prerequisites that landed first."

create_and_conclude \
  "Review the detached sessions implementation quality" \
  "Code quality strong — clean structure, ~3000 lines of tests, good separation of concerns, thorough error handling with retry + DLQ. Identified integration gaps in tool server boot timing, session registration, and session-running/session-record event whitelisting."

create_and_conclude \

  "Shipped. BlockType.check() returns a richer signal indicating permanent unresolvability (patron rejection, cancelled dependency, deleted resource). Checker can fail the engine directly instead of requiring manual rig-resume."

input.content:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

SESSION="bae3f417-1473-4b6c-a4e9-fcd9c6c8d384"
cd /workspace/vibers

create_click() {
  local goal="$1"
  local parent="${2:-}"
  local args=(--goal "$goal" --created-session-id "$SESSION")
  if [[ -n "$parent" ]]; then
    args+=(--parent-id "$parent")
  fi
  local id
  id=$(nsg click create "${args[@]}" 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.id')
  echo "$id"
  echo "  ● $id  ${goal:0:70}" >&2
}

echo "=== CLUSTER 1: Unlocking Autonomous Operation ===" >&2

ROOT_AUTO=$(create_click "Unlock autonomous hopper-based operation for Nexus")

# Decision-centric planner subtree
PLANNER=$(create_click "Redesign Astrolabe planner for intent-and-constraints specs with tiered decision model" "$ROOT_AUTO")
create_click "Design the preference model that graduates patron corrections into planning defaults" "$PLANNER"
create_click "Design tiered decision model that proceeds on best-guess defaults" "$PLANNER"
HANDOFF=$(create_click "Design task session handoff notes for the implement-loop engine" "$PLANNER")
create_click "Where should handoff notes be stored — task writ, session record, or dedicated book?" "$HANDOFF"
create_click "Should all previous handoff notes be queryable, or just the immediately prior one?" "$HANDOFF"

# Seal-engine rebase conflicts
REBASE=$(create_click "Choose recovery policy for seal-engine rebase conflicts" "$ROOT_AUTO")
create_click "Discard-and-recommission vs reconcile-engine vs stuck-escalation — which?" "$REBASE"

# Reviewer rarely rejects
REVIEWER=$(create_click "Why does the reviewer rarely reject?" "$ROOT_AUTO")
create_click "Is the reviewer too weak, or are specs tight enough that pass-through is legitimate?" "$REVIEWER"

# Concurrency control
CONCUR=$(create_click "Design concurrency-control substrate for parallel planning rigs" "$ROOT_AUTO")
create_click "Design OCC validation of plans against observed read sets" "$CONCUR"
create_click "Resolve plan-vs-plan conflicts when two Astrolabe sessions overlap" "$CONCUR"
create_click "Design intake tier model (backlog / ready pool / parked) to decouple dump from dispatch" "$CONCUR"
create_click "Design load-bearing blocked_by links as scheduler prerequisites" "$CONCUR"

# Task decomposition
TASKDEC=$(create_click "Replace narrative-blob specs with structured task decomposition and acceptance verification" "$ROOT_AUTO")
create_click "Produce prompt guidance for verify-command quality" "$TASKDEC"
create_click "Design multi-turn conversational analyst mode for plan refinement" "$TASKDEC"

# Multi-rig refactor
MULTIRIG=$(create_click "Separate writs (obligations) from rigs (attempts) — the multi-rig refactor" "$ROOT_AUTO")
create_click "Untangle the three meanings overloaded onto parent-child relationships" "$MULTIRIG"
create_click "Design cross-rig data flow and workspace persistence" "$MULTIRIG"
create_click "Design rig template generation beyond one template per writ type" "$MULTIRIG"
create_click "Should brief → mandate collapse into a single writ under multi-rig?" "$MULTIRIG"

# Standalone children of autonomous operation
create_click "Design background monitoring of in-flight commissions (Vigil)" "$ROOT_AUTO"
create_click "Design first-class internal commission flow (Petition)" "$ROOT_AUTO"
create_click "Design cost tracking and token budget allocation (Coinmaster/Purse/Tithe)" "$ROOT_AUTO"

echo "" >&2
echo "=== CLUSTER 2: Astrolabe Agility ===" >&2

ROOT_AGILITY=$(create_click "Cut Astrolabe per-commission time and cost for autonomous batch operation")

ATLAS=$(create_click "Design GSD-intel-style atlas as pre-built interpretive context for Astrolabe" "$ROOT_AGILITY")
create_click "Design Layer 1: prose preamble from LLM mapper with brief-type → subset mapping" "$ATLAS"
create_click "Design Layer 2: deterministic repository facts from non-LLM tooling" "$ATLAS"
create_click "Design Layer 3: staleness detection, refresh policy, atlas-as-engine-precondition" "$ATLAS"

READER=$(create_click "Profile and cut reader stage full-inventory cost" "$ROOT_AGILITY")
create_click "Where do Astrolabe's time and tokens actually go across stages?" "$READER"

echo "" >&2
echo "=== CLUSTER 3: Click Implementation (live items only) ===" >&2

create_click "Design Coco scope-closure discipline: explore/decide modes + context checkpoints"
create_click "Design top-level click orientation as a focusing tool"

echo "" >&2
echo "=== CLUSTER 4: Quest System → Decisions (one surviving item) ===" >&2

create_click "Design first-class queryable decision records with attribution and ratification flow"

echo "" >&2
echo "=== CLUSTER 5: Containerized Anima Sessions ===" >&2

ROOT_CONTAINER=$(create_click "Run every anima session in its own container for isolation and parallel safety")
create_click "Achieve deterministic session directory isolation — container vs flag hardening" "$ROOT_CONTAINER"

echo "" >&2
echo "=== CLUSTER 6: Standalone Root Clicks ===" >&2

create_click "Design Clockworks MVP timer apparatus"
create_click "Scope daemon end-to-end integration test coverage"
create_click "Diagnose MCP tool-server 500s dropping mid-session on astrolabe reader"
create_click "Design engine-declared MCP tool preconditions with pre-session verification"
create_click "Design session consult — entering an existing autonomous session interactively"
create_click "Should engine completion be predicated on tool-call trace rather than session exit?"
create_click "Design recovery from daemon restarts that strand engines in running state"

# X013 instrumentation mini-umbrella
X013=$(create_click "Track X013 quality scorer open issues")
create_click "Design unified instrument context for cache efficiency" "$X013"
create_click "Design structured concern lists on quality scorers (QS-2)" "$X013"

create_click "Rethink commission-log granularity when briefs spawn multiple mandates"
create_click "Codify the explicit-contracts principle — make implicit shared understanding into upfront artifacts"
create_click "Evaluate work-tracking primitives beyond writs+clicks (backlog, seeds, threads)"
create_click "Evaluate explicit design contracts as first-class planning artifacts beyond UI"
create_click "Adopt assumptions mode as the default interaction style for design conversations"
create_click "Design prompt-injection hardening for commission-sourced prompts"

echo "" >&2
echo "=== ACTIVE MIGRATION COMPLETE ===" >&2

input.command:
cd /workspace/vibers && for id in $(nsg writ list --type quest --limit 200 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.[].id'); do
  nsg writ show "$id" 2>/dev/null | jq '.' > "/workspace/nexus-mk2/docs/archive/quests/${id}.json"
  echo "  archived $id"
done

content.text: Now let me also grab the file-canonical quest bodies that have richer content than the DB rows:

input.description: Check how many quest body files exist

content.text: 89 quest writs archived as JSON. The file-canonical bodies (in `/workspace/vibers/writs/quests/`) have richer content for the quests that used that convention — but the JSON dumps include whatever was in the DB row's body field, which for many active quests is just a stub pointing at the file. Let me merge the file bodies into the JSON archives where they exist:

input.command:
cd /workspace/vibers && for f in writs/quests/w-*.md; do
  id=$(basename "$f" .md)
  json_file="/workspace/nexus-mk2/docs/archive/quests/${id}.json"
  if [[ -f "$json_file" ]]; then
    body=$(cat "$f")
    # Use jq to replace the body field with the file-canonical body
    jq --arg body "$body" '.body = $body | .bodySource = "file-canonical"' "$json_file" > "${json_file}.tmp" && mv "${json_file}.tmp" "$json_file"
    echo "  enriched $id"
  fi
done
